Cause: Only OWNER can change roles

Solution: You have EDITOR role. Contact workspace owner to request role change for another member.

docs.title: "Can't delete workspace even though I'm OWNER"
docs.text:
Possible causes: 1. Workspace has pending operations (wait for completion) 2. Browser issue (refresh and try again)

Solution: Try from Workspace Settings → Danger Zone → Delete Workspace

docs.title: Scenario: Editing a Request
docs.text:
OWNER sees: 

URL:        [https://api.example.com/users     ] (editable)
Method:     [GET ▼]                             (editable)
Headers:    [+ Add Header]                      (editable)
Body:       [JSON editor]                       (editable)

[Send Request]  [Save]  [Delete]

EDITOR sees: 

URL:        [https://api.example.com/users     ] (editable)
Method:     [GET ▼]                             (editable)
Headers:    [+ Add Header]                      (editable)
Body:       [JSON editor]                       (editable)

[Send Request]  [Save]  [Delete]

VIEWER sees: 

URL:        https://api.example.com/users        (read-only, gray)
Method:     GET                                   (read-only)
Headers:    Authorization: Bearer ***            (read-only)
Body:       { "name": "John" }                    (read-only)

[Copy to Personal Workspace]

docs.title: Scenario: Viewing Workspace Settings
docs.text:
OWNER sees: 

Workspace Settings

General:
  Name:         [Backend API Team        ] (editable)
  Description:  [Team collaboration...  ] (editable)
  [Save Changes]

Members:
  Alice Johnson  OWNER   [Change Role ▼] [Remove]
  Bob Smith      EDITOR  [Change Role ▼] [Remove]
  [+ Invite Member]

Danger Zone:
  [Delete Workspace]

EDITOR sees: 

Workspace Settings

General:
  Name:         Backend API Team            (read-only)
  Description:  Team collaboration...       (read-only)

Members:
  Alice Johnson  OWNER
  Bob Smith      EDITOR  (you)
  Carol White    VIEWER

VIEWER sees: 

Workspace Settings

General:
  Name:         Backend API Team            (read-only)
  Description:  Team collaboration...       (read-only)

Members:
  Alice Johnson  OWNER
  Bob Smith      EDITOR
  Carol White    VIEWER  (you)

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• Workspace Sharing - Invite members and collaborate
  • Workspaces - Workspace fundamentals
  • Environments - Manage environment variables
  • Collections - Organize requests in shared collections
  • Import/Export - Share collections with external teams

docs.title: Workspace Sharing
docs.text: Workspaces in Simba allow teams to collaborate on API testing by sharing collections, environments, and requests. Invite team members and control their access with role-based permissions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
What is Workspace Sharing?

Workspaces are shared containers that hold: - Collections: Folders and requests organized by project - Environments: Variable sets for different deployment stages - Request History: Team's API execution logs - Data Files: CSV/JSON files for data-driven testing

Use cases: - Team collaboration: Multiple developers testing same API - QA activities: Testers validate functionality - API documentation: Share working examples with stakeholders - Knowledge sharing: Onboard new team members with ready-to-use collections - Cross-functional work: Backend, frontend, QA collaborate on single source of truth

docs.title: Personal vs. Shared Workspaces
docs.text:
Personal Workspace: - Created automatically for each user - Private by default (only you can see) - Perfect for personal testing, experiments - Can be shared by inviting members

Shared Workspace: - Explicitly created for team collaboration - Has multiple members with different roles - Owner controls access and permissions -All changes visible to team members with appropriate access

docs.title: Create New Workspace
docs.text:
1. Click Workspaces dropdown in top-left
  2. Click "+ New Workspace"
  3. Enter details:
  4. Name: Backend API Team (descriptive, clear)
  5. Description: Shared collections for backend microservices testing
  6. Settings: SSL verification, proxy configuration (optional)
  7. Click "Create"
  8. Workspace created with you as OWNER

docs.title: Switch Between Workspaces
docs.text:
1. Click workspace name in top-left
  2. Select from dropdown: 
    My Workspace (Personal)
Backend API Team (Shared)
Frontend Testing (Shared)
QA Automation (Shared)
  3. All collections, environments change to selected workspace

docs.title: Edit Workspace Settings
docs.text:
1. Click workspace name → "Workspace Settings"
  2. Modify:
  3. Name and description
  4. SSL certificate validation
  5. Proxy settings
  6. Default environment
  7. Click "Save Changes"

Note: Only OWNER role can edit workspace settings

docs.title: Send Invitation
docs.text:
1. Open workspace you want to share
  2. Click workspace name → "Manage Members"
  3. Click "+ Invite Member"
  4. Enter team member's email address
  5. Select role: OWNER, EDITOR, or VIEWER (see Permissions page)
  6. Optionally add message: 
    "Welcome to the team! This workspace contains our backend API tests."
  7. Click "Send Invitation"
  8. Invitation email sent to recipient

docs.title: Accept Invitation (Recipient)
docs.text:
1. Recipient receives email: "You've been invited to join [Workspace Name]"
  2. Click "Accept Invitation" link in email
  3. If no Simba account:
  4. Redirected to Sign Up page
  5. Create account
  6. Automatically added to workspace after sign up
  7. If existing account:
  8. Login
  9. Added to workspace immediately
  10. Workspace appears in workspace dropdown

docs.title: Pending Invitations
docs.text:
View pending invitations: 

Workspace Settings → Members tab

Pending Invitations:
  john@example.com      EDITOR    Sent: 2024-03-14  [Resend] [Cancel]
  jane@example.com      VIEWER    Sent: 2024-03-12  [Resend] [Cancel]

Actions: - Resend: Send invitation email again - Cancel: Revoke invitation (link becomes invalid)

docs.title: View Current Members
docs.text:
Workspace: Backend API Team
Members: 5

Owner:
  Alice Johnson (alice@company.com)      OWNER      Joined: 2024-01-10

Editors:
  Bob Smith (bob@company.com)            EDITOR     Joined: 2024-01-15
  Carol White (carol@company.com)        EDITOR     Joined: 2024-02-01

Viewers:
  Dan Brown (dan@company.com)            VIEWER     Joined: 2024-02-20
  Eve Green (eve@company.com)            VIEWER     Joined: 2024-03-01

docs.title: Change Member Role
docs.text:
1. Go to Workspace Settings → Members
  2. Find member in list
  3. Click role dropdown next to their name
  4. Select new role: OWNER, EDITOR, VIEWER
  5. Confirm change
  6. Member's permissions updated immediately

Note: Only OWNER can change roles

docs.title: Remove Member
docs.text:
1. Go to Workspace Settings → Members
  2. Find member in list
  3. Click "Remove" button (🗑️ icon)
  4. Confirm: "Are you sure you want to remove [Name] from this workspace?"
  5. Click "Remove"
  6. Member loses access immediately
  7. Workspace no longer appears in their workspace list

Note: Only OWNER can remove members

docs.title: Workflow 1: Team API Development
docs.text:
Scenario: Backend team developing new API

Setup: 

Workspace: Payment API Development
Members:
  - Sarah (OWNER) - Tech Lead
  - Mark (EDITOR) - Backend Developer
  - Lisa (EDITOR) - QA Engineer
  - Tom (VIEWER) - Frontend Developer

Workflow: 1. Sarah creates workspace and base collections 2. Mark adds new endpoints as he develops them 3. Lisa adds comprehensive test scripts 4. Tom views collections to understand API contracts (read-only) 5. All changes synced in real-time

docs.title: Workflow 2: QA Testing Team
docs.text:
Scenario: QA team testing staging environment

Setup: 

Workspace: QA Staging Tests
Members:
  - QA Manager (OWNER)
  - 3 QA Engineers (EDITOR)
  - Product Manager (VIEWER)

Workflow: 1. QA Manager sets up environments (Dev, Staging, Prod) 2. QA Engineers create test collections 3. QA Engineers run collections, share results 4. Product Manager reviews test coverage (read-only)

docs.title: Workflow 3: API Documentation
docs.text:
Scenario: Sharing working API examples with stakeholders

Setup: 

Workspace: Customer API Examples
Members:
  - API Team (OWNER/EDITOR) - Maintain examples
  - Customers (VIEWER) - View example requests

Workflow: 1. API team creates collections with example requests 2. Add descriptions and comments to requests 3. Invite customers as VIEWER 4. Customers can load examples, modify locally, but not change shared version

docs.title: Live Updates
docs.text:
When a team member makes changes, you see updates in real-time:

✨ Bob Smith created collection "Order Management"
✨ Carol White added request "Create Order"
✨ Dan Brown updated environment "Staging" variables

docs.title: Activity Feed
docs.text:
View workspace activity: 

Workspace Settings → Activity tab

Today:
  10:30 AM  Bob Smith       Created request "Get User"
  9:45 AM   Carol White     Updated collection "Users"
  9:00 AM   Alice Johnson   Added member "eve@company.com"

Yesterday:
  5:15 PM   Bob Smith       Ran collection "Smoke Tests"
  2:30 PM   Carol White     Created environment "Production"

docs.title: Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
Simultaneous edits: 

You: Editing request "Create User"
Carol: Also editing request "Create User"

Notification: "Carol is also editing this request"

Last save wins: - Simba shows warning if multiple people edit same request - Last person to save overwrites previous changes - No automatic merging (like Git)

Best practice: - Communicate with team before editing shared requests - Use comments to indicate work in progress - Consider creating duplicate for experimentation

docs.title: Strategy 1: Workspace per Project
docs.text:
My Workspaces:
  📁 Mobile App API (iOS/Android team)
  📁 Web Dashboard API (Web team)
  📁 Admin Portal API (Admin team)
  📁 Public API (External developers)

Pros: Clear separation, focused collaboration Cons: More workspaces to manage

docs.title: Strategy 2: Workspace per Environment
docs.text:
My Workspaces:
  📁 Development (All Dev testing)
  📁 Staging (Pre-prod validation)
  📁 Production (Prod monitoring)

Pros: Clear environment separation Cons: Duplicate collections across workspaces

docs.title: Strategy 3: Workspace per Team
docs.text:
My Workspaces:
  📁 Engineering Team (Dev + QA)
  📁 Product Team (PMs + Stakeholders)
  📁 Customer Success (Support team)

Pros: Aligned with org structure Cons: May need cross-team access

docs.title: Recommended Structure
docs.text:
Recommended: 

📁 Backend API - Development
  ├── Collections: Auth, Users, Orders, etc.
  ├── Environments: Local, Dev
  └── Members: Dev team (EDITOR), QA (EDITOR)

📁 Backend API - Staging
  ├── Collections: Same as Dev
  ├── Environments: Staging
  └── Members: QA (EDITOR), Product (VIEWER)

📁 Backend API - Production
  ├── Collections: Read-only monitoring
  ├── Environments: Prod (limited members)
  └── Members: SRE team only (VIEWER primarily)

docs.title: Access Control
docs.text:
Members can only access what their role permits:

Action OWNER EDITOR VIEWER View collections ✅ ✅ ✅ View environments ✅ ✅ ✅ View history ✅ ✅ ✅ Create/edit collections ✅ ✅ ❌ Create/edit environments ✅ ✅ ❌ Send requests ✅ ✅ ❌ Invite members ✅ ❌ ❌ Change member roles ✅ ❌ ❌ Delete workspace ✅ ❌ ❌

See Permissions for detailed role breakdown.

docs.title: Sensitive Data Protection
docs.text: Best practices: - Store sensitive values in environment variables (not visible in UI by default) - Use separate workspaces for different security levels - Grant VIEWER role to external stakeholders - Regularly audit workspace members (remove inactive users) - Use production workspaces sparingly (read-only monitoring only)

docs.title: Audit Trail
docs.text:
All workspace actions are logged: 

Audit Log (Admin only):
  2024-03-15 10:30  Bob added to workspace as EDITOR
  2024-03-14 16:45  Carol created collection "Orders"
  2024-03-14 09:00  Dan sent 50 requests to Production
  2024-03-13 14:20  Eve's role changed from EDITOR to VIEWER

docs.title: As a Member (Non-Owner)
docs.text:
1. Go to Workspace Settings → Members
  2. Find yourself in members list
  3. Click "Leave Workspace"
  4. Confirm: "You will lose access to all collections and environments"
  5. Workspace removed from your list

docs.title: As the Owner
docs.text:
Cannot leave if you're the only OWNER: 

Error: "You cannot leave this workspace because you are the only owner.
       Transfer ownership or delete the workspace."

To leave: 1. Promote another member to OWNER 2. Then click "Leave Workspace"

docs.title: Deleting a Workspace
docs.text: ⚠️ Warning: Deleting a workspace is permanent and cannot be undone.

docs.title: Delete Workspace
docs.text:
1. Workspace Settings → Danger Zone
  2. Click "Delete Workspace"
  3. Confirm by typing workspace name: Backend API Team
  4. Click "Delete Permanently"
  5. Workspace and ALL its data deleted:
  6. Collections
  7. Environments
  8. Request history
  9. Data files
  10. Member associations

Note: Only OWNER can delete workspace

docs.title: Before Deleting
docs.text: Backup your data: 1. Export all collections (see Import/Export) 2. Export environments 3. Download data files 4. Take screenshots of important configurations

docs.title: Invitation Not Received
docs.text: Solutions: 1. Check spam/junk folder 2. Verify correct email address 3. Resend invitation from workspace settings 4. Contact workspace owner to confirm invitation sent

docs.title: Can't Edit Collection (VIEWER Role)
docs.text:
Cause: VIEWER role is read-only

Solutions: 1. Request workspace owner to change your role to EDITOR 2. Copy collection to your personal workspace (changes won't sync)

docs.title: Changes Not Syncing
docs.text: Solutions: 1. Refresh Simba application 2. Check internet connection 3. Verify you have EDITOR role (not VIEWER) 4. Check if another member is editing simultaneously

docs.title: Member Not Showing in Workspace
docs.text: Solutions: 1. Member must accept invitation first 2. Check pending invitations list 3. Resend invitation if expired 4. Verify member logged into correct Simba account

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• Permissions - Detailed role-based access control
  • Workspaces - Core workspace concepts
  • Environments - Share environment configurations
  • Collections - Organize shared requests
  • Import/Export - Backup and share collections

docs.title: Collections
docs.text: Collections are groups of related API requests organized in a hierarchical structure. They help you organize, share, and run multiple requests together.

docs.title: What is a Collection?
docs.text:
A collection is a folder-like container that holds:

  • Requests: Individual API endpoints
  • Folders: Nested sub-collections for organization
  • Variables: Collection-scoped variables
  • Scripts: Shared pre-request and test logic
  • Authentication: Inherited authentication settings

📁 User Management API
   ├─ 📋 Collection Variables
   ├─ 🔐 Collection Authentication
   ├─ 📝 Collection Scripts
   │
   ├─ 📁 Users
   │   ├─ GET Get All Users
   │   ├─ GET Get User by ID
   │   ├─ POST Create User
   │   ├─ PUT Update User
   │   └─ DELETE Delete User
   │
   ├─ 📁 Authentication
   │   ├─ POST Login
   │   ├─ POST Register
   │   └─ POST Refresh Token
   │
   └─ 📁 Admin
       ├─ 📁 User Management
       │   ├─ GET List Users
       │   └─ PUT Update User Role
       └─ 📁 System
           ├─ GET System Status
           └─ POST Clear Cache

docs.title: From the Sidebar
docs.text:
To create a collection:

  1. Click + New button in the sidebar
  2. Select New Collection
  3. Fill in the form:
  4. Name: e.g., "User Management API"
  5. Description: e.g., "Endpoints for managing users"
  6. Click Create

The collection appears in the sidebar and is ready for requests.

docs.title: Quick Collection from Request
docs.text:
To create a collection with a request:

  1. Click + New → New Request
  2. In the dialog:
  3. Name: Request name
  4. Collection: Select + Create New Collection
  5. Enter collection name
  6. Click Create

Both the collection and request are created together.

docs.title: Hierarchy
docs.text:
Collections support unlimited nesting:

Collection (Root)
├─ Folder Level 1
│  ├─ Folder Level 2
│  │  ├─ Folder Level 3
│  │  │  └─ Request
│  │  └─ Request
│  └─ Request
└─ Request

Best Practice

Keep hierarchy shallow (2-3 levels) for easier navigation.

docs.title: Request Types
docs.text:
Collections can contain different request types:

Type Icon Purpose REST 🌐 HTTP requests (GET, POST, PUT, etc.) GraphQL ◆ GraphQL queries and mutations WebSocket ⚡ WebSocket connections

docs.title: Create New Request
docs.text:
Method 1: Right-Click Menu

  1. Right-click collection or folder
  2. Select Add Request
  3. Fill in:
  4. Name: e.g., "Get All Users"
  5. Method: GET, POST, PUT, etc.
  6. URL: API endpoint
  7. Click Create

Method 2: + New Button

  1. Click + New → New Request
  2. Select target collection/folder
  3. Fill in request details
  4. Click Create

docs.title: Duplicate Request
docs.text:
To duplicate an existing request:

  1. Right-click the request
  2. Select Duplicate Request
  3. New request appears with "(Copy)" suffix
  4. Rename and modify as needed

docs.title: Move Request
docs.text:
To move a request between folders:

  1. Right-click the request
  2. Select Move to Folder
  3. Choose destination folder
  4. Click Move

Or simply drag and drop requests in the tree.

docs.title: Collection-Level Configuration
docs.text: Collections support shared settings that apply to all requests within them.

docs.title: Collection Variables
docs.text:
Variables defined at the collection level are available to all requests in that collection.

To add collection variables:

  1. Right-click collection
  2. Select Collection Settings
  3. Go to Variables tab
  4. Add variables:
Variable Initial Value Current Value apiVersion v1 v1 userId 12345 12345 timeout 5000 5000
  1. Click Save

Usage in requests: 

{{apiVersion}}/users/{{userId}}

Variable Resolution Order: 1. Environment variables (highest priority) 2. Collection variables 3. Global variables (lowest priority)

Learn more about variables →

docs.title: Collection Authentication
docs.text:
Set authentication once at the collection level instead of repeating it for every request.

To configure collection auth:

  1. Right-click collection → Collection Settings
  2. Go to Authorization tab
  3. Select auth type:
  4. Bearer Token
  5. Basic Auth
  6. API Key
  7. OAuth 2.0
  8. AWS Signature V4
  9. Enter credentials
  10. Click Save

Inheritance: - ✅ All requests in the collection inherit this auth - ✅ Individual requests can override collection auth - ✅ Folders can override for their requests

Learn more about authentication →

docs.title: Collection Scripts
docs.text:
Write scripts that run for every request in the collection.

Pre-Request Script (runs before every request): 

// Set timestamp for all requests
pm.collectionVariables.set('timestamp', Date.now());

// Log request details
console.log('Executing:', pm.info.requestName);

// Refresh auth token if expired
const tokenExpiry = pm.collectionVariables.get('tokenExpiry');
if (Date.now() > tokenExpiry) {
    // Fetch new token logic here
}

Test Script (runs after every request): 

// Verify all responses have 2xx status
pm.test("Status is success", function() {
    pm.response.to.be.success;
});

// Log response time
console.log('Response time:', pm.response.responseTime, 'ms');

// Track API usage
const requestCount = pm.collectionVariables.get('requestCount') || 0;
pm.collectionVariables.set('requestCount', requestCount + 1);

Script Execution Order: 1. Collection pre-request script 2. Folder pre-request script (if in folder) 3. Request pre-request script 4. REQUEST SENT ⚡ 5. Request test script 6. Folder test script (if in folder) 7. Collection test script

docs.title: Folders
docs.text:
Create folders to group related requests.

To create a folder:

  1. Right-click collection
  2. Select Add Folder
  3. Enter folder name: e.g., "Authentication"
  4. Click Create

To create a subfolder:

  1. Right-click a folder
  2. Select Add Subfolder
  3. Enter name
  4. Click Create

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use clear, descriptive names:

Collections: 

✅ User Management API
✅ Payment Service v2
✅ E-commerce Backend

❌ API
❌ Collection 1
❌ Test

Folders: 

✅ Authentication
✅ User CRUD
✅ Admin Functions

❌ Folder 1
❌ New Folder
❌ Misc

Requests: 

✅ GET Get All Users
✅ POST Create User
✅ PUT Update User by ID

❌ Request 1
❌ Test
❌ Get

docs.title: Reordering
docs.text:
To reorder requests/folders:

Method 1: Drag & Drop - Click and drag items up/down - Drop in new position

Method 2: Context Menu - Right-click item - Select Move Up or Move Down

docs.title: Running Collections
docs.text: Execute all requests in a collection sequentially using the Collection Runner.

docs.title: Running a Collection
docs.text:
To run a collection:

  1. Right-click collection
  2. Select Run Collection
  3. Configure run:
  4. Environment: Choose active environment
  5. Iterations: Number of times to run (for data-driven testing)
  6. Delay: Milliseconds between requests
  7. Data File: Upload CSV/JSON for data-driven tests
  8. Click Run

Runner executes: - All requests in order - Pre-request scripts - Test scripts - Captures results

Results include: - ✅ Passed tests - ❌ Failed tests - ⏱️ Response times - 📊 Success rate - 📝 Execution logs

Learn more about Collection Runner →

docs.title: Running a Folder
docs.text:
Run only requests within a specific folder:

  1. Right-click folder
  2. Select Run Folder
  3. Configure and run

This executes only the requests within that folder (and subfolders).

docs.title: Export Collection
docs.text:
To export a collection:

  1. Right-click collection
  2. Select Export
  3. Choose format:
  4. Simba Format: Full collection with scripts
  5. Postman v2.1: Compatible with Postman
  6. Click Export
  7. Save JSON file

Export includes: - All requests and folders - Collection variables - Pre-request and test scripts - Authentication settings

Export excludes: - Environment variables - Request history - Workspace members

docs.title: Import Collection
docs.text:
To import a collection:

  1. Click Import button (sidebar)
  2. Select source:
  3. Simba JSON: From Simba export
  4. Postman Collection: From Postman
  5. OpenAPI/Swagger: Generate from API spec
  6. Upload file
  7. Select target workspace
  8. Click Import

Postman Migration

Simba fully supports Postman Collection v2.1 format for easy migration.

Learn more about import/export →

docs.title: Share with Team
docs.text:
Share collections with workspace members:

  1. Ensure team members are added to workspace
  2. Collections are automatically visible to all members
  3. Member permissions control what they can do:
  4. Viewers: Can view and execute
  5. Editors: Can modify requests
  6. Admins: Can delete collections

Learn more about permissions →

docs.title: Collection Settings
docs.text:
Access comprehensive collection configuration.

To open collection settings:

  1. Right-click collection
  2. Select Collection Settings

docs.title: General Tab
docs.text:
• Name: Update collection name
  • Description: Markdown-supported description
  • Created Date: When collection was created
  • Request Count: Number of requests in collection

docs.title: Variables Tab
docs.text: Manage collection-scoped variables.

docs.title: Authorization Tab
docs.text: Set collection-level authentication that all requests inherit.

docs.title: Pre-request Script Tab
docs.text: JavaScript code that runs before every request in the collection.

docs.title: Test Script Tab
docs.text: JavaScript code that runs after every request in the collection.

docs.title: Settings Tab
docs.text:
• Request timeout: Default timeout for all requests
  • Follow redirects: Auto-follow HTTP redirects
  • SSL verification: Enable/disable SSL certificate verification

docs.title: Organization
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Group related endpoints in collections - Use folders for logical grouping - Keep collections focused on one API/service - Use consistent naming across collections

❌ Don't: - Mix unrelated APIs in one collection - Create too many nested folders (keep it 2-3 levels) - Duplicate requests across collections - Use vague names like "Test" or "Collection 1"

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Use collection variables for API-specific values - Document variable purpose in descriptions - Use environment variables for stage-specific values - Set sensible default values

❌ Don't: - Hardcode values that change between environments - Use collection variables for secrets (use environments) - Override variables without documenting why - Create unused variables

docs.title: Scripts
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Use collection scripts for shared logic - Add descriptive test names - Log important information to console - Handle errors gracefully

❌ Don't: - Duplicate logic across request scripts - Write tests without assertions - Leave debug console.log() statements - Ignore script errors

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Remove outdated requests regularly - Update descriptions when APIs change - Version your collections (export with version in name) - Test collections periodically

❌ Don't: - Keep broken requests - Let collections grow indefinitely - Forget to update after API changes - Skip testing before sharing

docs.title: RESTful CRUD Collection
docs.text:
📁 User API
   ├─ GET List Users (?page=1&limit=10)
   ├─ POST Create User
   ├─ GET Get User by ID (/:id)
   ├─ PUT Update User (/:id)
   ├─ PATCH Partial Update (/:id)
   └─ DELETE Delete User (/:id)

docs.title: Microservices Collection
docs.text:
📁 E-commerce Platform
   ├─ 📁 User Service
   │   ├─ Authentication
   │   └─ Profile Management
   ├─ 📁 Product Service
   │   ├─ Product CRUD
   │   └─ Inventory
   ├─ 📁 Order Service
   │   ├─ Create Order
   │   ├─ Order Status
   │   └─ Order History
   └─ 📁 Payment Service
       ├─ Process Payment
       └─ Refund

docs.title: Workflow Collection
docs.text:
📁 User Registration Flow
   ├─ 1. POST Register User
   ├─ 2. POST Verify Email
   ├─ 3. POST Login
   ├─ 4. GET Get User Profile
   └─ 5. PUT Update Profile

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• :material-variable:{ .lg .middle } Variables

    Use collection and environment variables effectively

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Variables Guide

  • :material-shield-key:{ .lg .middle } Authentication

    Configure collection-level authentication

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Authentication Guide

  • :material-play-box-multiple:{ .lg .middle } Collection Runner

    Run entire collections for automated testing

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Collection Runner Guide

  • :material-export:{ .lg .middle } Import/Export

    Share collections and migrate from other tools

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Import/Export Guide

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
??? question "How many requests can a collection have?" There's no limit, but for performance, we recommend keeping collections under 100 requests. Use folders to organize large collections.

??? question "Can I nest folders infinitely?" Yes, but we recommend 2-3 levels maximum for ease of navigation.

??? question "Do collection scripts run for every request?" Yes, collection scripts run for all requests unless overridden at the request level.

??? question "Can I duplicate a collection?" Export the collection and import it back with a new name.

??? question "How do I delete multiple requests at once?" Delete the folder containing them, or select multiple requests with Cmd/Ctrl+Click and press Delete.

??? question "Can different collections use the same request?" No, each request belongs to one collection. Duplicate the request if needed in multiple places.

??? question "Do collection variables override environment variables?" No, environment variables have higher priority than collection variables.

??? question "Can I share a collection without sharing the workspace?" Yes, export the collection as JSON and share the file. Others can import it into their own workspace.

docs.title: Environments
docs.text: Environments are named sets of variables that represent different stages of your API (Development, Staging, Production). They allow you to switch between contexts without changing your requests.

docs.title: What is an Environment?
docs.text:
An environment is a collection of key-value pairs (variables) that represent a specific context:

Example: Development Environment 

{
  "name": "Development",
  "variables": [
    { "key": "baseUrl", "value": "http://localhost:3000" },
    { "key": "apiKey", "value": "dev_key_12345" },
    { "key": "timeout", "value": "5000" }
  ]
}

Example: Production Environment 

{
  "name": "Production",
  "variables": [
    { "key": "baseUrl", "value": "https://api.example.com" },
    { "key": "apiKey", "value": "prod_key_98765" },
    { "key": "timeout", "value": "30000" }
  ]
}

Same request works in both: 

GET {{baseUrl}}/api/users
Headers:
  X-API-Key: {{apiKey}}

docs.title: Switch Contexts Easily
docs.text: Test the same requests across Development, Staging, and Production without editing URLs or credentials.

docs.title: Prevent Mistakes
docs.text: Avoid accidentally hitting production APIs during development.

docs.title: Share Safely
docs.text: Share collections with colleagues without exposing production credentials (use environment-specific values).

docs.title: Manage Complexity
docs.text: Handle different URLs, ports, API keys, and configurations per environment.

docs.title: From Environment Selector
docs.text:
To create an environment:

  1. Click Environment dropdown (top center)
  2. Select + Create Environment
  3. Fill in the form:
  4. Name: e.g., "Development"
  5. Description (optional): "Local development environment"
  6. Click Create

The new environment appears in the dropdown.

docs.title: Adding Variables
docs.text:
To add variables:

  1. Select the environment from dropdown
  2. Click ⚙️ Edit icon
  3. Add variables:
Variable Initial Value Current Value baseUrl http://localhost:3000 http://localhost:3000 apiKey dev_api_key_12345 dev_api_key_12345 username testuser testuser timeout 5000 5000
  1. Click Save

Initial vs Current Value

  • Initial Value: Checked into version control, shared with team
  • Current Value: Local only, not shared (for temporary overrides)

docs.title: Switching Environments
docs.text:
To switch environments:

  1. Click Environment dropdown (top center)
  2. Select the environment to activate

Visual indicator: Active environment name appears in the dropdown.

When you switch: - ✅ All requests use the new environment's variables - ✅ {{variable}} placeholders are resolved from the selected environment - ✅ Previous environment is deactivated

docs.title: No Environment
docs.text: Select No Environment to: - Use only collection and global variables - Test without environment-specific values - Debug variable resolution issues

docs.title: Environment Quick Look
docs.text: Hover over the 👁️ Quick Look icon to see all variables in the active environment without opening the editor.

docs.title: Development Environment
docs.text:
For local development and testing:

{
  "name": "Development",
  "variables": [
    { "key": "baseUrl", "value": "http://localhost:3000" },
    { "key": "apiKey", "value": "dev_key" },
    { "key": "dbName", "value": "test_db" },
    { "key": "logLevel", "value": "debug" },
    { "key": "timeout", "value": "5000" }
  ]
}

Characteristics: - Points to localhost - Uses test credentials - Short timeouts for fast feedback - Debug logging enabled

docs.title: Staging Environment
docs.text:
For pre-production testing:

{
  "name": "Staging",
  "variables": [
    { "key": "baseUrl", "value": "https://staging-api.example.com" },
    { "key": "apiKey", "value": "staging_key" },
    { "key": "dbName", "value": "staging_db" },
    { "key": "logLevel", "value": "info" },
    { "key": "timeout", "value": "10000" }
  ]
}

Characteristics: - Points to staging server - Uses staging credentials - Medium timeouts - Info-level logging

docs.title: Production Environment
docs.text:
For live API testing:

{
  "name": "Production",
  "variables": [
    { "key": "baseUrl", "value": "https://api.example.com" },
    { "key": "apiKey", "value": "prod_key" },
    { "key": "dbName", "value": "prod_db" },
    { "key": "logLevel", "value": "error" },
    { "key": "timeout", "value": "30000" }
  ]
}

Characteristics: - Points to production server - Uses production credentials - Long timeouts for reliability - Error-only logging

Production Safety

Be extremely careful when testing against production. Consider: - Read-only requests (GET) only - Test data that can be safely deleted - Rate limiting considerations - Backup before destructive operations

docs.title: In Request URL
docs.text:
GET {{baseUrl}}/api/users/{{userId}}

Simba replaces {{baseUrl}} and {{userId}} with values from the active environment.

docs.title: In Headers
docs.text:
Headers:
  Authorization: Bearer {{authToken}}
  X-API-Key: {{apiKey}}
  X-Environment: {{environmentName}}

docs.title: In Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "username": "{{testUsername}}",
  "email": "{{testEmail}}",
  "apiKey": "{{apiKey}}"
}

docs.title: In Query Parameters
docs.text: ?api_key={{apiKey}}&limit={{defaultLimit}}&page={{currentPage}}

docs.title: In Scripts
docs.text:
Pre-Request Script: 

// Get variable
const baseUrl = pm.environment.get('baseUrl');
console.log('Testing against:', baseUrl);

// Set variable
pm.environment.set('timestamp', Date.now());

Test Script: 

// Extract from response and save
const token = pm.response.json().token;
pm.environment.set('authToken', token);

// Use in next request
const userId = pm.response.json().userId;
pm.environment.set('testUserId', userId);

Learn more about variable syntax →

docs.title: Updating Variables
docs.text:
In Environment Editor: 1. Click Environment dropdown → ⚙️ Edit 2. Modify variable values 3. Click Save

In Scripts: 

// Update existing variable
pm.environment.set('authToken', newToken);

// Remove variable
pm.environment.unset('oldVariable');

docs.title: Sensitive Variables
docs.text:
For secrets (passwords, API keys):

Best Practices: - ✅ Use Current Value (not shared) - ✅ Don't commit to version control - ✅ Use environment-specific secrets - ✅ Rotate regularly

In Environment Editor:

Variable Initial Value Current Value Type apiKey (empty) actual_secret_key secret

Current Value is used but not exported or shared.

Security

Never commit production credentials to version control. Use Current Values or external secret managers.

docs.title: Variable Types
docs.text:
Type Usage Example String URLs, names https://api.example.com Number IDs, ports 3000 Boolean Flags true Secret Passwords, keys •••••••

Secret variables are masked in the UI and not exported.

docs.title: Duplicating Environments
docs.text:
Create similar environments quickly:

To duplicate an environment:

  1. Select environment to duplicate
  2. Click ⚙️ Edit → ⋮ More → Duplicate
  3. Enter new name: e.g., "Development (Backup)"
  4. Modify variables as needed
  5. Click Save

Use cases: - Create Staging from Production (with different URLs) - Create personal dev environment from team dev environment - Backup before major changes

docs.title: Exporting
docs.text:
To export an environment:

  1. Select environment
  2. Click ⚙️ Edit → ⋮ More → Export
  3. Save JSON file

Export includes: - Environment name - Initial values (not current values) - Variable descriptions

Export excludes: - Current values (for security) - Sensitive secrets

docs.title: Importing
docs.text:
To import an environment:

  1. Click Import button (top bar)
  2. Select Environment
  3. Upload JSON file
  4. Click Import

Sharing Environments

Export environments to share with team members. They'll need to add their own secrets in Current Value.

docs.title: When to Use Environment Variables
docs.text:
Use Environment Variables for: - ✅ Values that change between Dev/Staging/Prod - ✅ API endpoints and URLs - ✅ Authentication tokens - ✅ Database names - ✅ Feature flags per environment

Example: 

{
  "baseUrl": "https://staging-api.example.com",
  "apiKey": "staging_key_12345",
  "dbName": "staging_db"
}

docs.title: When to Use Collection Variables
docs.text:
Use Collection Variables for: - ✅ Values that don't change between environments - ✅ API-specific constants - ✅ Default values - ✅ Temporary values during collection runs

Example: 

{
  "apiVersion": "v2",
  "defaultLimit": "10",
  "contentType": "application/json"
}

docs.title: Variable Priority
docs.text:
Variables are resolved in this order (highest to lowest):

  1. Environment Variables (highest)
  2. Collection Variables
  3. Global Variables (lowest)

If {{userId}} exists in both environment and collection, environment value wins.

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use clear, descriptive names:

✅ Good: 

baseUrl
authToken
apiKey
databaseHost
maxRetries

❌ Bad: 

url
token
key
host
max

docs.title: Consistent Structure
docs.text:
Keep the same variables across all environments:

Development: 

{ "baseUrl": "http://localhost:3000", "apiKey": "dev_key" }

Staging: 

{ "baseUrl": "https://staging.api.com", "apiKey": "stg_key" }

Production: 

{ "baseUrl": "https://api.com", "apiKey": "prod_key" }

All have baseUrl and apiKey - only values differ.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Protect sensitive data:

✅ Do: - Use Current Value for secrets - Don't commit secrets to version control - Rotate credentials regularly - Use different keys per environment - Limit who can access production environments

❌ Don't: - Store passwords in Initial Value - Share production credentials in exported files - Use the same API key across environments - Leave credentials in exported collections

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Document your variables:

[
  {
    "key": "baseUrl",
    "value": "http://localhost:3000",
    "description": "API base URL for all endpoints"
  },
  {
    "key": "apiKey",
    "value": "dev_key_12345",
    "description": "API authentication key (rotate monthly)"
  },
  {
    "key": "timeout",
    "value": "5000",
    "description": "Request timeout in milliseconds"
  }
]

docs.title: Dynamic Variables
docs.text:
Set variables dynamically in scripts:

Pre-Request Script: 

// Generate timestamp
pm.environment.set('timestamp', Date.now());

// Generate random ID
pm.environment.set('requestId', Math.random().toString(36).substring(7));

// Calculate auth signature
const signature = calculateSignature(pm.environment.get('apiKey'));
pm.environment.set('authSignature', signature);

docs.title: Chaining Requests
docs.text:
Pass data between requests using environment variables:

Request 1: Login 

// Test Script
const response = pm.response.json();
pm.environment.set('authToken', response.token);
pm.environment.set('userId', response.user.id);

Request 2: Get User Profile 

GET {{baseUrl}}/users/{{userId}}
Authorization: Bearer {{authToken}}

docs.title: Conditional Logic
docs.text:
Use environment-specific logic:

const env = pm.environment.name;

if (env === 'Production') {
    // Production-only checks
    pm.test("Response under 100ms", function() {
        pm.expect(pm.response.responseTime).to.be.below(100);
    });
} else {
    // Dev/Staging: Detailed debug info
    console.log('Full response:', pm.response.json());
}

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• :material-variable:{ .lg .middle } Variables

    Complete guide to variable types and usage

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Variables Guide

  • :material-folder:{ .lg .middle } Collections

    Use collection variables alongside environments

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Collections Guide

  • :material-code-braces:{ .lg .middle } Scripting

    Set and get variables programmatically

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Scripting API Reference

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
??? question "How many environments can I create?" There's no limit. Create as many as you need for different stages or configurations.

??? question "Can I have an environment active in multiple workspaces?" No, environments are workspace-scoped. Each workspace has its own environments.

??? question "What happens if I delete an active environment?" Simba switches to "No Environment" and requests will fail if they depend on environment variables.

??? question "Can team members see my Current Values?" No, Current Values are local to your machine and never shared or exported.

??? question "How do I share environments with my team?" Environments are shared automatically within a workspace. All members can see and use them (based on permissions).

??? question "Can I version control environments?" Yes, export environments as JSON and commit to git. Don't commit secrets (use Current Values).

??? question "What's the difference between Initial and Current Value?" Initial Value is shared/exported, Current Value is local and not shared (use for secrets).

??? question "Can I use environment variables in another environment's variables?" No, variable nesting is not supported. Each variable is independent.

docs.title: Requests
docs.text: Requests are the core building blocks of API testing in Simba. Each request represents a single API call with all its configuration: URL, method, headers, body, authentication, and scripts.

docs.title: What is a Request?
docs.text:
A request is a configured API call that contains:

  • HTTP Method: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, etc.
  • URL: API endpoint with optional variables
  • Headers: HTTP headers (Content-Type, Authorization, etc.)
  • Query Parameters: URL parameters (?key=value)
  • Body: Request payload (JSON, form-data, XML, etc.)
  • Authentication: Auth configuration
  • Scripts: Pre-request and test scripts

docs.title: Request Types
docs.text: Simba supports three types of requests:

docs.title: REST API Requests 🌐
docs.text:
Standard HTTP requests with full method support: - GET - Retrieve data - POST - Create new resources - PUT - Replace resources - PATCH - Update resources - DELETE - Remove resources - HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE

Learn more about REST requests →

docs.title: GraphQL Requests ◆
docs.text:
GraphQL queries and mutations with: - Query editor - Variables support - Schema introspection - Fragment support

Learn more about GraphQL →

docs.title: WebSocket Connections ⚡
docs.text:
Real-time WebSocket testing: - Connect to WebSocket servers - Send and receive messages - Monitor connection status

Learn more about WebSocket →

docs.title: Quick Create
docs.text:
To create a request:

  1. Right-click a collection or folder
  2. Select Add Request
  3. Fill in basic details:
  4. Name: Descriptive name
  5. Request Type: REST, GraphQL, or WebSocket
  6. Method: GET, POST, etc. (for REST)
  7. URL: API endpoint
  8. Click Create

docs.title: From Scratch
docs.text:
To create without a collection first:

  1. Click + New → New Request
  2. If no collection exists, you'll be prompted to create one
  3. Fill in request details
  4. Click Create

docs.title: URL
docs.text:
The request URL can contain:

Static URLs: 

https://api.example.com/users

URLs with variables: 

{{baseUrl}}/api/v{{apiVersion}}/users/{{userId}}

URLs with path parameters: 

{{baseUrl}}/users/:id

Variable Hover

Hover over {{variable}} to see its current value.

docs.title: HTTP Methods
docs.text: Method Purpose Has Body GET Retrieve data ❌ No POST Create resources ✅ Yes PUT Replace resources ✅ Yes PATCH Partial update ✅ Yes DELETE Remove resources ⚠️ Optional HEAD Get headers only ❌ No OPTIONS Get allowed methods ❌ No

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
Add parameters that appear after ? in the URL.

Key Value Description Enabled page 1 Page number ✅ limit 10 Items per page ✅ sort name Sort field ✅ filter active Filter by status ❌

Resulting URL: 

https://api.example.com/users?page=1&limit=10&sort=name

Disabled parameters are ignored.

Learn more about query parameters →

docs.title: Headers
docs.text:
HTTP headers provide metadata about the request.

Common Headers:

Header Purpose Example Content-Type Request body format application/json Accept Response format application/json Authorization Authentication Bearer {{token}} User-Agent Client identifier Simba/1.0 X-API-Key API key {{apiKey}}

Auto-generated Headers: - Content-Length: Automatically calculated - Host: From URL - Connection: Managed by Simba

Learn more about headers →

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text: Configure the request payload for POST, PUT, PATCH methods.

docs.title: JSON Body
docs.text:
{
  "name": "John Doe",
  "email": "john@example.com",
  "role": "admin",
  "metadata": {
    "department": "Engineering",
    "level": {{userLevel}}
  }
}

Features: - Syntax highlighting - Auto-formatting (Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + F) - Variable support - Validation

docs.title: Form Data
docs.text:
Key Value Type Description username johndoe Text Username avatar [Select File] File Profile picture bio Software Engineer Text User bio

Use for: File uploads, multipart/form-data requests

docs.title: Other Body Types
docs.text:
• Raw: Plain text, XML, HTML, JavaScript
  • Binary: Direct file upload
  • GraphQL: Query/mutation editor

Learn more about request bodies →

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Configure authentication at the request level or inherit from collection.

docs.title: Auth Types
docs.text:
Bearer Token: 

Authorization: Bearer {{authToken}}

Basic Auth: 

Authorization: Basic base64(username:password)

API Key: - Header: X-API-Key: {{apiKey}} - Query: ?api_key={{apiKey}}

OAuth 2.0: - Full OAuth flow support - Token management

Learn more about authentication →

docs.title: Scripts
docs.text: Add JavaScript code to run before/after requests.

docs.title: Pre-Request Scripts
docs.text:
Code that runs before the request is sent:

// Set dynamic timestamp
pm.environment.set('timestamp', Date.now());

// Generate request ID
pm.environment.set('requestId', Math.random().toString(36).substring(7));

// Refresh token if expired
const tokenExpiry = pm.environment.get('tokenExpiry');
if (Date.now() > tokenExpiry) {
    // Token refresh logic
}

// Log request details
console.log('Sending request to:', pm.request.url);

Common uses: - Set dynamic variables - Generate timestamps - Refresh authentication tokens - Calculate signatures - Prepare request data

docs.title: Test Scripts
docs.text:
Code that runs after receiving the response:

// Test 1: Status code
pm.test("Status code is 200", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

// Test 2: Response time
pm.test("Response time under 500ms", function() {
    pm.expect(pm.response.responseTime).to.be.below(500);
});

// Test 3: JSON structure
pm.test("Response has required fields", function() {
    const data = pm.response.json();
    pm.expect(data).to.have.property('id');
    pm.expect(data).to.have.property('name');
    pm.expect(data.name).to.be.a('string');
});

// Extract data for next request
const userId = pm.response.json().id;
pm.environment.set('extractedUserId', userId);

Common uses: - Validate response status - Assert response structure - Extract data for next requests - Calculate metrics - Conditional logic

Learn more about scripts →

docs.title: Execute Request
docs.text:
To send a request:

  1. Configure the request (URL, method, headers, body)
  2. Click Send button (or press Cmd/Ctrl + Enter)
  3. Wait for response
  4. View results in Response Viewer

docs.title: Request Flow
docs.text:
1. Execute Pre-Request Script
        ↓
2. Resolve Variables ({{variable}})
        ↓
3. Apply Authentication
        ↓
4. Build HTTP Request
        ↓
5. Send Request ⚡
        ↓
6. Receive Response
        ↓
7. Execute Test Script
        ↓
8. Display Results
        ↓
9. Save to History

docs.title: Response Status
docs.text: Status Information: - Status Code: 200 OK, 404 Not Found, 500 Error, etc. - Response Time: Milliseconds - Response Size: KB or MB

docs.title: Response Body
docs.text:
View response data in different formats:

Pretty (formatted): 

{
  "id": 1,
  "name": "John Doe",
  "email": "john@example.com",
  "address": {
    "city": "New York",
    "country": "USA"
  }
}

Raw (unformatted): 

{"id":1,"name":"John Doe","email":"john@example.com","address":{"city":"New York","country":"USA"}}

Preview (rendered HTML): Displays HTML responses as rendered web pages.

docs.title: Response Headers
docs.text:
View all headers returned by the server:

content-type: application/json; charset=utf-8
content-length: 1234
x-ratelimit-limit: 100
x-ratelimit-remaining: 95
cache-control: no-cache

docs.title: Test Results
docs.text: See test execution results: - ✅ Passed tests (with execution time) - ❌ Failed tests (with error details) - Summary: X/Y tests passed

docs.title: Request History
docs.text:
Every request execution is saved to history.

History includes: - Timestamp - Method and URL - Status code - Response time - Full request/response details

To view history: 1. Click History tab in sidebar 2. Browse past executions 3. Click any entry to view details 4. Re-run previous requests

Learn more about history →

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use descriptive names that explain what the request does:

✅ Good: 

GET Get All Users
POST Create New User  
PUT Update User by ID
DELETE Remove User

❌ Bad: 

Request 1
Test
Get Users
Update

docs.title: Folder Structure
docs.text:
Group related requests in folders:

📁 User Management
   ├─ GET List Users
   ├─ GET Get User by ID
   ├─ POST Create User
   ├─ PUT Update User
   └─ DELETE Delete User

docs.title: Request Descriptions
docs.text:
Add descriptions to document: - What the request does - Required parameters - Expected responses - Business logic

Example: 

## Get User by ID

Retrieves a single user by their unique ID.

**Path Parameters:**
- `id` (required): User's unique identifier

**Response:**
- `200`: User found and returned
- `404`: User not found
- `401`: Unauthorized

docs.title: URL Structure
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Use variables for base URLs: {{baseUrl}}/users - Use variables for dynamic segments: /users/{{userId}} - Use query params for filters: ?status=active&page=1

❌ Don't: - Hardcode URLs: https://api.example.com/users - Mix path params and query params inconsistently

docs.title: Headers
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Set Content-Type for POST/PUT requests - Use variables for auth tokens: Bearer {{token}} - Inherit common headers from collection - Disable unused headers instead of deleting

❌ Don't: - Duplicate headers across requests - Hardcode sensitive values - Leave outdated headers enabled

docs.title: Request Bodies
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Format JSON properly (use auto-format) - Use variables for dynamic data - Validate JSON syntax before sending - Document required fields in description

❌ Don't: - Send invalid JSON - Include unnecessary fields - Hardcode test data - Use production data in shared collections

docs.title: Scripts
docs.text:
✅ Do: - Use descriptive test names - Add error handling - Log important information - Clean up temporary variables

❌ Don't: - Leave debug console.log everywhere - Write tests without assertions - Ignore script errors - Set sensitive data in logs

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• :material-web:{ .lg .middle } REST API Requests

    Complete guide to REST API testing

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: REST Guide

  • :material-graphql:{ .lg .middle } GraphQL Requests

    Test GraphQL APIs with queries and mutations

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: GraphQL Guide

  • :material-shield-key:{ .lg .middle } Authentication

    Configure request authentication

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Auth Guide

  • :material-code-braces:{ .lg .middle } Scripts

    Write pre-request and test scripts

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Scripting Guide

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
??? question "Can I reuse requests across collections?" No, each request belongs to one collection. Duplicate the request if needed in multiple collections.

??? question "How do I save a request?" Requests auto-save as you make changes. Click the Save button or press Cmd/Ctrl + S to force save.

??? question "Can I send multiple requests at once?" Use the Collection Runner to send multiple requests sequentially.

??? question "What happens if a request times out?" The request fails with a timeout error. Increase timeout in request settings or environment variables.

??? question "Can I download response bodies?" Yes, click the download icon in the response body tab to save the response to a file.

??? question "How do I duplicate a request?" Right-click the request and select Duplicate Request.

??? question "Can I compare responses?" View request history to see previous responses and compare them manually.

??? question "Do scripts have access to filesystem?" No, scripts run in a sandboxed environment with no filesystem access for security.

docs.title: Variables
docs.text: Variables are dynamic placeholders that store values you can reuse across requests, scripts, and collections. They use the {{variableName}} syntax and make your API tests flexible and maintainable.

docs.title: What are Variables?
docs.text:
Variables are key-value pairs that can be used anywhere in Simba:

  • Request URLs
  • Headers
  • Query parameters
  • Request body
  • Scripts
  • Authentication

Example: 

URL: {{baseUrl}}/users/{{userId}}
Header: Authorization: Bearer {{authToken}}
Body: { "key": "{{apiKey}}" }

When executed, Simba replaces {{variables}} with their actual values.

docs.title: Variable Scopes
docs.text: Simba has three variable scopes with different priorities:

docs.title: 1. Environment Variables
docs.text:
Scope: Environment-specific (Dev, Staging, Production) Priority: Highest Use for: Values that change between environments

{
  "baseUrl": "https://api-dev.example.com",
  "apiKey": "dev_key_12345",
  "timeout": "5000"
}

Learn more about environments →

docs.title: 2. Collection Variables
docs.text:
Scope: Collection-specific (all requests in a collection) Priority: Medium Use for: Collection-wide constants

{
  "apiVersion": "v2",
  "defaultLimit": "10",
  "contentType": "application/json"
}

Learn more about collections →

docs.title: 3. Global Variables
docs.text:
Scope: Workspace-wide (available everywhere) Priority: Lowest Use for: Truly global values

{
  "workspaceId": "12345",
  "timestamp": "1704398400000"
}

docs.title: Variable Resolution Order
docs.text:
When Simba encounters {{variableName}}, it searches in this order:

1. Environment Variables (highest priority)
   ↓ if not found
2. Collection Variables
   ↓ if not found
3. Global Variables (lowest priority)
   ↓ if not found
4. {{variableName}} (left as-is)

Example:

// Environment has: userId = "123"
// Collection has: userId = "456"
// Global has: userId = "789"

{{userId}}  // Resolves to "123" (environment wins)

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Use double curly braces {{variableName}}:

{{baseUrl}}
{{apiKey}}
{{userId}}
{{timestamp}}

docs.title: In Different Contexts
docs.text:
URL: 

GET {{baseUrl}}/api/v{{apiVersion}}/users/{{userId}}

Headers: 

Authorization: Bearer {{authToken}}
X-API-Key: {{apiKey}}
X-Request-ID: {{requestId}}

Query Parameters: 

?api_key={{apiKey}}&page={{page}}&limit={{limit}}

Request Body (JSON): 

{
  "username": "{{username}}",
  "email": "{{email}}",
  "apiKey": "{{apiKey}}",
  "timestamp": {{timestamp}}
}

Request Body (Form Data): 

Key: api_key
Value: {{apiKey}}

docs.title: Multiple Variables
docs.text:
Use multiple variables in one string:

{{protocol}}://{{host}}:{{port}}/{{path}}

// Resolves to:
https://api.example.com:443/v1/users

docs.title: Nested Paths
docs.text:
Variables resolve to complete values (no nesting):

✅ {{baseUrl}}/users
✅ {{protocol}}://{{host}}

❌ {{base{{env}}Url}}  // Nesting not supported

docs.title: In Environment/Collection Editor
docs.text:
Environment Variables: 1. Click Environment dropdown → ⚙️ Edit 2. Add variable: - Key: baseUrl - Value: https://api.example.com 3. Click Save

Collection Variables: 1. Right-click collection → Collection Settings 2. Go to Variables tab 3. Add variable 4. Click Save

docs.title: In Scripts
docs.text:
Variables can be set dynamically in pre-request and test scripts.

Environment Variables: 

// Set
pm.environment.set('variableName', 'value');

// Get
const value = pm.environment.get('variableName');

// Remove
pm.environment.unset('variableName');

// Check existence
if (pm.environment.has('variableName')) {
    // Variable exists
}

Collection Variables: 

// Set
pm.collectionVariables.set('variableName', 'value');

// Get
const value = pm.collectionVariables.get('variableName');

// Remove
pm.collectionVariables.unset('variableName');

Global Variables: 

// Set
pm.globals.set('variableName', 'value');

// Get
const value = pm.globals.get('variableName');

// Remove
pm.globals.unset('variableName');

Dynamic Variables (during script execution): 

// Set temporary variable (highest priority)
pm.variables.set('tempVar', 'value');

// Get with fallback
const value = pm.variables.get('variableName'); // Checks all scopes

docs.title: 1. Dynamic Authentication
docs.text:
Pre-Request Script (Login request): 

// Request body has username/password
// Response contains token

Test Script (Login request): 

// Extract token from response
const response = pm.response.json();
pm.environment.set('authToken', response.access_token);
pm.environment.set('refreshToken', response.refresh_token);

console.log('Token saved for subsequent requests');

Next Request (Get User): 

GET {{baseUrl}}/users/me
Authorization: Bearer {{authToken}}

docs.title: 2. Request Chaining
docs.text:
Extract data from one response to use in the next request.

Request 1: Create User 

// Test Script
const user = pm.response.json();
pm.environment.set('userId', user.id);
pm.environment.set('userEmail', user.email);

Request 2: Get User Details 

GET {{baseUrl}}/users/{{userId}}

Request 3: Update User 

PUT {{baseUrl}}/users/{{userId}}
Body: { "email": "{{userEmail}}" }

docs.title: 3. Timestamps and Random Data
docs.text:
Pre-Request Script: 

// Current timestamp
pm.environment.set('timestamp', Date.now());

// ISO date string
pm.environment.set('isoDate', new Date().toISOString());

// Random string
pm.environment.set('randomId', Math.random().toString(36).substring(7));

// Random number
pm.environment.set('randomNum', Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000));

// UUID (simple version)
pm.environment.set('uuid', 
    'xxxxxxxx-xxxx-4xxx-yxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx'.replace(/[xy]/g, function(c) {
        const r = Math.random() * 16 | 0;
        const v = c === 'x' ? r : (r & 0x3 | 0x8);
        return v.toString(16);
    })
);

Usage: 

{
  "id": "{{uuid}}",
  "timestamp": {{timestamp}},
  "createdAt": "{{isoDate}}",
  "key": "{{randomId}}"
}

docs.title: 4. Computed Values
docs.text:
Calculate values based on other variables:

Pre-Request Script: 

const basePrice = pm.collectionVariables.get('basePrice');
const taxRate = pm.environment.get('taxRate');
const total = basePrice * (1 + taxRate);

pm.variables.set('totalPrice', total);

Request Body: 

{
  "basePrice": {{basePrice}},
  "taxRate": {{taxRate}},
  "total": {{totalPrice}}
}

docs.title: 5. Conditional Variables
docs.text:
Set variables based on conditions:

Pre-Request Script: 

const env = pm.environment.name;

if (env === 'Production') {
    pm.variables.set('logLevel', 'error');
    pm.variables.set('timeout', 30000);
} else {
    pm.variables.set('logLevel', 'debug');
    pm.variables.set('timeout', 5000);
}

docs.title: 6. Counter Variables
docs.text:
Track iterations or counts:

Test Script: 

// Increment counter
let count = pm.collectionVariables.get('requestCount') || 0;
count++;
pm.collectionVariables.set('requestCount', count);

console.log(`Request #${count} completed`);

docs.title: Built-in Dynamic Variables
docs.text:
Simba provides special variables that generate values automatically:

Variable Description Example {{$timestamp}} Current Unix timestamp (seconds) 1704398400 {{$isoTimestamp}} Current ISO 8601 timestamp 2024-01-15T12:00:00.000Z {{$randomInt}} Random integer (0-1000) 742 {{$guid}} Random GUID/UUID a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 {{$randomAlphaNumeric}} Random alphanumeric string (8 chars) aB3xQ9tL

Usage: 

{
  "id": "{{$guid}}",
  "timestamp": {{$timestamp}},
  "createdAt": "{{$isoTimestamp}}",
  "code": "{{$randomAlphaNumeric}}"
}

Regeneration

Dynamic variables generate new values each time the request is sent.

docs.title: Viewing Variables
docs.text:
Quick Look: - Hover over {{variable}} in editor to see its value - Click 👁️ icon in environment dropdown

Environment Editor: - See all environment variables with values - Edit, add, or remove variables

Console: 

// List all environment variables
console.log('Environment:', pm.environment.toObject());

// List all collection variables
console.log('Collection:', pm.collectionVariables.toObject());

// List all global variables
console.log('Global:', pm.globals.toObject());

docs.title: Clearing Variables
docs.text:
Clear all environment variables: 

pm.environment.clear();

Clear all collection variables: 

pm.collectionVariables.clear();

Clear specific variables: 

pm.environment.unset('authToken');
pm.environment.unset('userId');
pm.collectionVariables.unset('tempData');

docs.title: Bulk Operations
docs.text:
Set multiple variables: 

const variables = {
    'baseUrl': 'https://api.example.com',
    'apiKey': 'key_12345',
    'timeout': 5000
};

Object.entries(variables).forEach(([key, value]) => {
    pm.environment.set(key, value);
});

Copy variables between scopes: 

// Copy from environment to collection
const authToken = pm.environment.get('authToken');
pm.collectionVariables.set('authToken', authToken);

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use descriptive, consistent names:

✅ Good: 

baseUrl
authToken
userId
apiKey
requestTimeout

❌ Bad: 

url
token
id
key
timeout

Conventions: - Use camelCase: userName, apiKey, authToken - Be descriptive: userAuthToken not token - Use prefixes for related vars: api_key, api_version, api_timeout

docs.title: Variable Organization
docs.text:
Environment Variables (change per environment): - URLs and endpoints - API keys and credentials - Environment-specific settings - Database connection strings

Collection Variables (constant per collection): - API versions - Default values - Configuration constants - Schema definitions

Global Variables (truly global): - Workspace-wide settings - Shared utilities - Cross-collection data

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Protect sensitive data:

✅ Do: - Use environment variables for secrets - Use "Current Value" for sensitive data (not shared) - Clear sensitive variables after use - Don't log secrets to console

❌ Don't: - Hardcode credentials in requests - Store production secrets in "Initial Value" - Commit sensitive variables to version control - Share environments with secrets

Example: 

// ✅ Clear secret after use
pm.test("Store temporary token", function() {
    const token = pm.response.json().token;
    pm.variables.set('tempToken', token); // Only for this execution
});

// Later in test script
pm.variables.unset('tempToken'); // Clear immediately

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Document variables in environment/collection descriptions:

{
  "name": "Development",
  "description": "Local development environment",
  "variables": [
    {
      "key": "baseUrl",
      "value": "http://localhost:3000",
      "description": "API base URL for all endpoints"
    },
    {
      "key": "apiKey",
      "value": "dev_key_12345",
      "description": "Development API key (rotate monthly)"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Handle missing variables gracefully:

// Check before using
if (pm.environment.has('authToken')) {
    const token = pm.environment.get('authToken');
    // Use token
} else {
    console.error('Auth token not found. Please run login request first.');
}

// Provide defaults
const timeout = pm.environment.get('timeout') || 5000;
const limit = pm.variables.get('limit') || 10;

// Validate values
const apiKey = pm.environment.get('apiKey');
if (!apiKey || apiKey === '') {
    throw new Error('API key is required');
}

docs.title: View Current Values
docs.text:
In Console: 

console.log('Variable resolution:');
console.log('Environment:', pm.environment.get('userId'));
console.log('Collection:', pm.collectionVariables.get('userId'));
console.log('Global:', pm.globals.get('userId'));
console.log('Resolved:', pm.variables.get('userId')); // Final value

In Test Script: 

pm.test("Check variable values", function() {
    console.log('All environment vars:', pm.environment.toObject());
    console.log('All collection vars:', pm.collectionVariables.toObject());
    console.log('Active environment:', pm.environment.name);
});

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Variable not resolving: 

// Check if variable exists
if (!pm.variables.get('baseUrl')) {
    console.error('baseUrl not set in any scope');
}

// Check specific scope
if (!pm.environment.has('apiKey')) {
    console.warn('apiKey not in environment');
}

Wrong value being used: 

// Check resolution order
const value = pm.variables.get('userId');
console.log('Resolved userId:', value);
console.log('From environment?', pm.environment.get('userId') === value);
console.log('From collection?', pm.collectionVariables.get('userId') === value);

Variable not updating: 

// Verify it's being set
pm.environment.set('authToken', newToken);
console.log('Token after set:', pm.environment.get('authToken'));

// Check if it persists
pm.test("Variable persists", function() {
    pm.expect(pm.environment.get('authToken')).to.equal(newToken);
});

docs.title: Variable Chaining
docs.text:
Use variables to build complex URLs:

// Set parts
pm.environment.set('protocol', 'https');
pm.environment.set('subdomain', 'api');
pm.environment.set('domain', 'example.com');
pm.environment.set('port', '443');
pm.environment.set('path', 'v2/users');

// In request URL
{{protocol}}://{{subdomain}}.{{domain}}:{{port}}/{{path}}
// Resolves to: https://api.example.com:443/v2/users

docs.title: Variable Transformations
docs.text:
Transform variables before use:

// Uppercase
const username = pm.environment.get('username');
pm.variables.set('usernameUpper', username.toUpperCase());

// Encode for URL
const searchTerm = pm.environment.get('search');
pm.variables.set('searchEncoded', encodeURIComponent(searchTerm));

// Hash for signatures
const data = pm.environment.get('data');
// Use crypto library to hash
pm.variables.set('dataHash', hashFunction(data));

docs.title: Variable Arrays
docs.text:
Store arrays as JSON strings:

// Set array
const userIds = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
pm.environment.set('userIds', JSON.stringify(userIds));

// Get array
const userIds = JSON.parse(pm.environment.get('userIds'));
userIds.forEach(id => {
    console.log('User ID:', id);
});

docs.title: Variable Objects
docs.text:
Store complex objects:

// Set object
const config = {
    apiKey: 'key_12345',
    timeout: 5000,
    retries: 3
};
pm.collectionVariables.set('config', JSON.stringify(config));

// Get object
const config = JSON.parse(pm.collectionVariables.get('config'));
console.log('API Key:', config.apiKey);
console.log('Timeout:', config.timeout);

docs.text:

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Environments Guide

  • :material-folder:{ .lg .middle } Collections

    Use collection variables in your requests
