docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
??? question "Can variables reference other variables?" No, variable nesting like {{base{{env}}Url}} is not supported. Each variable resolves independently.

??? question "What happens if a variable isn't found?" The {{variableName}} placeholder remains as-is in the request, which usually causes the request to fail.

??? question "Can I use variables in scripts?" Yes, use pm.environment.get(), pm.collectionVariables.get(), or pm.variables.get().

??? question "How do I see which value will be used?" Hover over {{variable}} in the editor to see the resolved value and source scope.

??? question "Are variables case-sensitive?" Yes, {{userId}} and {{UserId}} are different variables.

??? question "Can I use special characters in variable names?" Variable names should use alphanumeric characters and underscores. Avoid spaces and special characters.

??? question "Do variables persist between sessions?" Environment and collection variables persist. Variables set with pm.variables.set() are temporary (execution-only).

??? question "Can I export variables?" Yes, environment and collection variables are exported. Current Values (secrets) are not exported.

docs.title: Workspaces
docs.text: Workspaces are the top-level organizational unit in Simba. Think of a workspace as a project container that holds all your collections, environments, and team members for a specific API testing project.

docs.title: What is a Workspace?
docs.text:
A workspace provides isolated containers for:

  • Collections: Groups of API requests
  • Environments: Variable sets for different stages (Dev, Staging, Production)
  • Team Members: Collaborators with specific permissions
  • History: Request execution logs
  • Settings: Workspace-specific configuration

docs.title: Isolation
docs.text: Each workspace is completely isolated from others. Changes in one workspace don't affect another.

docs.title: Organization
docs.text: Separate your work by: - Project: One workspace per API project - Client: One workspace per client - Environment: Development vs. Production testing - Team: Personal vs. team workspaces

docs.title: Collaboration
docs.text: Invite team members to specific workspaces and control their access levels.

docs.title: Data Separation
docs.text: Keep sensitive data (API keys, credentials) separate for different projects.

docs.title: From the UI
docs.text:
Method 1: First Login

When you first log in, Simba prompts you to create a workspace:

  1. Enter Workspace Name: e.g., "My API Project"
  2. Add Description (optional): e.g., "User management API testing"
  3. Click Create Workspace

Method 2: Workspace Switcher

  1. Click the Workspace dropdown (top left)
  2. Select + Create New Workspace
  3. Fill in the form:
  4. Name: Required, unique name
  5. Description: Optional details about the workspace
  6. Click Create

docs.title: Example Workspace Setup
docs.text:
🏢 User Management API
   Description: Testing endpoints for user CRUD operations
   Created: 2025-01-15
   Members: 3
   Collections: 5
   Environments: 3 (Dev, Staging, Prod)

docs.title: Switching Between Workspaces
docs.text:
You can work in multiple workspaces and switch between them easily.

To switch workspaces:

  1. Click the Workspace dropdown (top left)
  2. Select the workspace you want to open
  3. Simba loads that workspace's collections and environments

Keyboard Shortcut

Press Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + W to open the workspace switcher quickly.

When you switch workspaces: - ✅ All collections from the new workspace load - ✅ Environments are switched - ✅ Request tabs are closed (unsaved changes are lost) - ✅ History is filtered to the new workspace

docs.title: Workspace Settings
docs.text:
Manage workspace configuration and metadata.

To access settings:

  1. Click Workspace dropdown
  2. Select Workspace Settings

docs.title: General Settings
docs.text:
Name - Update workspace name - Must be unique across your workspaces

Description - Add or update workspace description - Supports markdown formatting

Created Date - Shows when workspace was created - Read-only

Owner - Shows who created the workspace - Cannot be changed

docs.title: Advanced Settings
docs.text:
Default Environment - Set which environment loads by default - Options: None, or any created environment

Auto-save Requests - Automatically save request changes - Reduces risk of losing work

Request Timeout - Default timeout for all requests in workspace - Can be overridden per request - Default: 30 seconds

docs.title: Workspace Members
docs.text: Collaborate with team members by inviting them to your workspace.

docs.title: Adding Members
docs.text:
To invite a team member:

  1. Go to Workspace Settings
  2. Click the Members tab
  3. Click + Invite Member
  4. Enter their email address
  5. Select permission level:
  6. Viewer: Read-only access
  7. Editor: Can create/edit requests
  8. Admin: Full control except deletion
  9. Click Send Invite

The invited user receives an email and appears in your workspace when they accept.

docs.title: Permission Levels
docs.text:
Permission Viewer Editor Admin Owner View collections ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ View environments ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ Execute requests ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ View history ✅ ✅ ✅ ✅ Create/edit requests ❌ ✅ ✅ ✅ Create/edit collections ❌ ✅ ✅ ✅ Create/edit environments ❌ ✅ ✅ ✅ Invite members ❌ ❌ ✅ ✅ Remove members ❌ ❌ ✅ ✅ Change permissions ❌ ❌ ✅ ✅ Edit workspace settings ❌ ❌ ✅ ✅ Delete workspace ❌ ❌ ❌ ✅

Permission Changes

Permission changes take effect immediately. Active sessions may need to refresh.

docs.title: Removing Members
docs.text:
To remove a member:

  1. Go to Workspace Settings → Members
  2. Find the member to remove
  3. Click the ⋮ menu next to their name
  4. Select Remove from Workspace
  5. Confirm removal

Immediate Effect

Removed members immediately lose access to the workspace and all its contents.

docs.title: Strategy 1: By Project
docs.text:
Create one workspace per API project:

🏢 E-commerce API
   ├─ User Service
   ├─ Product Service
   ├─ Order Service
   └─ Payment Service

🏢 Analytics Dashboard API
   ├─ Reports API
   ├─ Metrics API
   └─ Dashboard API

Best for: Multiple independent projects

docs.title: Strategy 2: By Client
docs.text:
Create one workspace per client:

🏢 Client A - Retail
   ├─ Production APIs
   ├─ Staging APIs
   └─ Internal Tools

🏢 Client B - Finance
   ├─ Core Banking API
   ├─ Mobile API
   └─ Web Portal API

Best for: Consultants, agencies, freelancers

docs.title: Strategy 3: By Environment
docs.text:
Create workspaces for different stages:

🏢 Development
   └─ All services (unstable)

🏢 Staging
   └─ All services (pre-release)

🏢 Production
   └─ All services (live)

Best for: Strict environment isolation

docs.title: Strategy 4: By Team
docs.text:
Create workspaces for different teams:

🏢 Backend Team
   ├─ Service 1
   ├─ Service 2
   └─ Database API

🏢 Frontend Team
   ├─ Web API Clients
   ├─ Mobile API Clients
   └─ Shared Components

🏢 QA Team
   ├─ Test Suites
   ├─ Regression Tests
   └─ Performance Tests

Best for: Large organizations

docs.title: Exporting a Workspace
docs.text:
Export your workspace to share with others or backup.

To export:

  1. Go to Workspace Settings
  2. Click Export tab
  3. Choose export format:
  4. Simba Format: Full workspace with history
  5. Postman Format: Compatible with Postman
  6. Click Export Workspace
  7. Save the JSON file

Export includes: - ✅ All collections and folders - ✅ All requests - ✅ Environment variables - ✅ Pre-request and test scripts - ❌ Request history (optional) - ❌ Workspace members (for security)

docs.title: Importing a Workspace
docs.text:
Import workspaces from Simba or migrate from Postman.

To import:

  1. Click Workspace dropdown
  2. Select Import Workspace
  3. Choose source:
  4. Simba JSON: From exported Simba workspace
  5. Postman Collection: From Postman export
  6. Select file to upload
  7. Click Import

Merge or Replace

When importing, you can: - Merge: Add to existing workspace - Create New: Create a new workspace for imported data

docs.title: Deleting a Workspace
docs.text:
Permanent Action

Deleting a workspace is permanent and cannot be undone. All collections, requests, environments, and history will be lost.

To delete a workspace:

  1. Go to Workspace Settings
  2. Scroll to Danger Zone
  3. Click Delete Workspace
  4. Type the workspace name to confirm
  5. Click Delete Forever

What gets deleted: - All collections and folders - All requests and history - All environments and variables - All member associations

What's preserved: - User accounts (members can still access other workspaces) - Exported backups (if you made them)

docs.title: Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Use clear, descriptive names:

✅ Good Examples: - "E-commerce Production API" - "User Service - Development" - "Client Portal APIs"

❌ Bad Examples: - "Test" - "My Workspace" - "Workspace 1"

docs.title: Workspace Structure
docs.text:
Keep workspaces focused:

✅ Do: - One workspace per project or client - Include all related API collections - Use consistent naming across workspaces

❌ Don't: - Mix unrelated projects in one workspace - Create too many workspaces for the same project - Duplicate collections across workspaces

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Protect sensitive data:

✅ Do: - Use environment variables for secrets - Limit member permissions appropriately - Export backups regularly - Review member list periodically

❌ Don't: - Hardcode API keys in requests - Give everyone admin access - Share workspace exports publicly - Keep removed employees as members

docs.title: Collaboration
docs.text:
Work effectively with your team:

✅ Do: - Document workspace purpose in description - Set default environment for consistency - Communicate changes to team members - Use collection-level scripts for shared logic

❌ Don't: - Make breaking changes without notice - Delete resources others are using - Override shared environments - Work in isolation without syncing

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• :material-folder-multiple:{ .lg .middle } Collections

    Learn how to organize requests into collections

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Collections Guide

  • :material-earth:{ .lg .middle } Environments

    Manage environment variables for different stages

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Environments Guide

  • :material-account-group:{ .lg .middle } Permissions

    Understand workspace permissions and roles

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Permissions Guide

  • :material-export:{ .lg .middle } Import/Export

    Share workspaces and migrate from other tools

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: Import/Export Guide

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
??? question "How many workspaces can I create?" There is no limit to the number of workspaces you can create. However, for better organization, we recommend creating workspaces only when needed.

??? question "Can I transfer workspace ownership?" Yes, as the owner, you can transfer ownership to another admin member in the workspace settings.

??? question "What happens if I leave a workspace?" If you're the owner, you must transfer ownership first. If you're a member, you'll lose access to the workspace and its contents.

??? question "Can I recover a deleted workspace?" No, workspace deletion is permanent. Always export a backup before deleting.

??? question "How do I rename a workspace?" Go to Workspace Settings → General → Edit Name → Save.

??? question "Can workspace members see each other?" Yes, all members can see the member list and their roles in Workspace Settings.

??? question "Are workspace environments shared?" Yes, environments are shared across all workspace members. Changes affect everyone.

??? question "Can I duplicate a workspace?" Export the workspace and import it as a new workspace with a different name.

docs.title: Your First Request
docs.text: Learn how to make your first API request in Simba in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1: Login
docs.text:
1. Open Simba at http://localhost:5174
  2. Login with your credentials or create a new account

Placeholder: Screenshot of login page

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Workspace
docs.text:
Workspaces organize your API testing projects.

  1. Click "+ New Workspace" in the sidebar
  2. Enter workspace details:
  3. Name: My First Workspace
  4. Description: Learning Simba basics

Placeholder: Screenshot showing workspace creation dialog

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Collection
docs.text:
Collections group related requests together.

  1. Right-click your workspace → "New Collection"
  2. Name it: "Getting Started"

Placeholder: Screenshot showing collection creation

docs.title: Step 4: Create Your First Request
docs.text:
Let's make a simple GET request to a public API.

  1. Right-click your collection → "New Request"
  2. Configure the request:

docs.title: Request Details
docs.text:
Method: GET
URL: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1
Name: Get User

Placeholder: Screenshot of request builder interface

docs.title: Step-by-Step
docs.text:
=== \"URL Bar\" 1. Select GET from the method dropdown 2. Enter URL: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1 3. Name your request: "Get User"

=== \"Headers (Optional)\" Headers are auto-added, but you can add custom ones: 

Key: Accept
Value: application/json

=== \"Send Request\" Click the blue "Send" button

docs.title: Step 5: View the Response
docs.text: After clicking Send, you'll see the response:

docs.title: Response Details
docs.text:
=== \"Body\" 

{
  \"id\": 1,
  \"name\": \"Leanne Graham\",
  \"username\": \"Bret\",
  \"email\": \"Sincere@april.biz\",
  \"address\": {
    \"street\": \"Kulas Light\",
    \"suite\": \"Apt. 556\",
    \"city\": \"Gwenborough\",
    \"zipcode\": \"92998-3874\"
  },
  \"phone\": \"1-770-736-8031 x56442\",
  \"website\": \"hildegard.org\"
}

=== \"Headers\" 

content-type: application/json; charset=utf-8
status: 200

=== \"Status\" - Status Code: 200 OK - Response Time: ~150ms - Size: 500 bytes

*Placeholder: Screenshot showing the response viewer

*

docs.title: Step 6: Save Your Request
docs.text:
1. Click the "Save" button in the top-right
  2. Your request is now saved in the collection

!!! tip \"Keyboard Shortcut\" Use Ctrl+S (or ⌘+S on Mac) to quickly save requests

docs.title: Step 7: Add a Test Script (Optional)
docs.text:
Let's verify the response with a test:

  1. Click the "Tests" tab
  2. Add this JavaScript code:
// Test that status is 200
pm.test(\"Status is 200\", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

// Test that response has user data
pm.test(\"Response has user name\", function() {
    const jsonData = pm.response.json();
    pm.expect(jsonData.name).to.exist;
    pm.expect(jsonData.name).to.equal(\"Leanne Graham\");
});

// Test response time
pm.test(\"Response time is less than 500ms\", function() {
    pm.expect(pm.response.responseTime).to.be.below(500);
});
  1. Click "Send" again
  2. Check the "Test Results" section

Placeholder: Screenshot showing passing tests

docs.title: Step 8: Try a POST Request
docs.text:
Let's create a new user (simulated):

  1. Create a new request: "Create User"
  2. Configure:
Method: POST
URL: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users
  1. Go to "Body" tab → Select "JSON"
  2. Add request body:
{
  \"name\": \"John Doe\",
  \"username\": \"johndoe\",
  \"email\": \"john@example.com\"
}
  1. Click "Send"

Expected response: 

{
  \"id\": 11,
  \"name\": \"John Doe\",
  \"username\": \"johndoe\",
  \"email\": \"john@example.com\"
}

docs.title: Common First-Time Questions
docs.text:
??? question \"Why is my request failing?\" Check: - ✅ URL is correct (include https://) - ✅ Internet connection is active - ✅ Server is reachable - ✅ CORS is configured (for local APIs)

??? question \"How do I save requests?\" Click the Save button or use Ctrl+S. Requests must be in a collection to be saved.

??? question \"Can I organize requests in folders?\" Yes! Right-click collection → New Folder, then drag requests into folders.

??? question \"Where is my request history?\" Click the History tab in the sidebar to see all recent requests.

docs.title: What You've Learned
docs.text: ✅ Created a workspace and collection ✅ Made a GET request ✅ Viewed and understood responses ✅ Added test scripts ✅ Made a POST request with body

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you've made your first request, explore:

  1. Interface Tour - Understand all UI elements
  2. Environment Variables - Use variables in requests
  3. Authentication - Add auth to requests
  4. GraphQL - Test GraphQL APIs

Interface Tour → Core Concepts →

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Get Simba up and running on your system in minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing Simba, ensure you have:

  • Node.js: Version 20.x or higher
  • PostgreSQL: Version 14 or higher
  • MongoDB: Version 6 or higher
  • Git: For cloning the repository

docs.title: Check Your Environment
docs.text:
node --version  # Should be v20.x or higher
npm --version
postgres --version
mongo --version

docs.title: Installation Methods
docs.text: Production DeploymentDevelopment SetupDocker Deployment

docs.title: Using Pre-built Package
docs.text:
1. Download the latest release 

    wget https://github.com/skckadiyala/APITestingTool/releases/latest/deploy.zip
unzip deploy.zip
cd deploy

  2. Configure environment variables 

    # Backend configuration
cd backend
cp .env.example .env
nano .env  # Edit with your database credentials

  3. Set up databases 

    # Create PostgreSQL database
createdb api_testing_tool

# Run migrations
cd backend
npx prisma migrate deploy
npx prisma generate

  4. Install dependencies and start 

    # Install backend dependencies
cd backend
npm install --production

# Install frontend dependencies
cd ../frontend
npm install --production

# Start with PM2
cd ..
pm2 start ecosystem.config.js

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository 

    git clone https://github.com/skckadiyala/APITestingTool.git
cd APITestingTool

  2. Start databases with Docker 

    docker-compose up -d

  3. Configure backend 

    cd backend
cp .env.example .env
# Edit .env with your settings
npm install
npx prisma migrate dev
npx prisma generate

  4. Configure frontend 

    cd ../frontend
cp .env.example .env
npm install

  5. Start development servers 

    # Terminal 1: Backend
cd backend
npm run dev

# Terminal 2: Frontend
cd frontend
npm run dev

  6. Access the application

  7. Frontend: http://localhost:5174
  8. Backend API: http://localhost:5000

docs.title: Using Docker Compose (Coming Soon)
docs.text: docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Backend .env
docs.text:
# Server Configuration
NODE_ENV=production
PORT=5000

# Database URLs
DATABASE_URL="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/api_testing_tool"
MONGODB_URI="mongodb://localhost:27017/api_testing_tool"

# JWT Secret (change in production!)
JWT_SECRET=your-super-secret-key
JWT_EXPIRES_IN=1d

# CORS Configuration
FRONTEND_URL=http://localhost:5174
CORS_ORIGIN=http://localhost:5174

docs.title: Frontend .env
docs.text:
# Backend API Configuration
VITE_API_BASE_URL=http://localhost:5000/api/v1
VITE_BACKEND_HOST=localhost
VITE_BACKEND_PORT=5000

Production Security

Always change JWT_SECRET to a strong random string in production: 

openssl rand -base64 32

docs.title: PostgreSQL
docs.text:
# Create database
createdb api_testing_tool

# Run migrations
cd backend
npx prisma migrate deploy

# Optional: Seed with sample data
npm run prisma:seed

docs.title: MongoDB
docs.text:
# MongoDB will auto-create database on first connection
# Optional: Seed with sample data
npm run seed:mongo

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
1. Check backend health 
    curl http://localhost:5000/health

Expected response: 

{
  "status": "success",
  "message": "API Testing Tool Backend is running",
  "timestamp": "2026-04-15T..."
}

  1. Check frontend

Open http://localhost:5174 in your browser. You should see the Simba login page.

  1. Create first user

Register a new account or use the seed user: - Email: admin@example.com - Password: password123

docs.title: Port Already in Use
docs.text:
# Find process using port 5000
lsof -i :5000
kill -9 <PID>

# Or change port in .env
PORT=5001

docs.title: Database Connection Failed
docs.text:
1. Verify databases are running: 

    ps aux | grep postgres
ps aux | grep mongod

  2. Check connection strings in .env

  3. Test connection: 

    psql -U user -d api_testing_tool
mongosh mongodb://localhost:27017/api_testing_tool

docs.title: Prisma Issues
docs.text:
# Regenerate Prisma client
npx prisma generate

# Reset database (WARNING: deletes data)
npx prisma migrate reset

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
✅ Installation complete!

Now you're ready to:

  1. Make your first API request
  2. Tour the interface
  3. Understand core concepts

First Request →

docs.title: Interface Tour
docs.text: This guide walks you through the Simba interface, explaining each section and how to use it effectively.

docs.title: Main Interface Layout
docs.text:
The main Simba interface with labeled sections

The Simba interface is divided into several key areas:

  1. Top Navigation Bar - Workspace switcher, environment selector, user menu
  2. Sidebar - Collection tree and navigation
  3. Request Builder - Configure and send requests
  4. Response Viewer - View and analyze responses
  5. Status Bar - Request status, response time, and size

docs.title: 1. Top Navigation Bar
docs.text: The top navigation bar provides quick access to essential features:

docs.title: Workspace Switcher
docs.text:
• Location: Top left corner
  • Purpose: Switch between different workspaces
  • Usage: Click to see all your workspaces, create new ones, or access workspace settings
🏢 My Workspace ▼
   ├─ My Workspace
   ├─ Team Project
   ├─ Personal APIs
   └─ + Create New Workspace

docs.title: Environment Selector
docs.text:
• Location: Top center
  • Purpose: Switch between environments (Dev, Staging, Production)
  • Usage: Select active environment to apply its variables to requests
🌍 Development ▼
   ├─ No Environment
   ├─ Development
   ├─ Staging
   ├─ Production
   └─ + Create Environment

docs.title: User Menu
docs.text:
• Location: Top right corner
  • Options:
  • 👤 Profile Settings
  • 🔑 Change Password
  • 📊 Usage Statistics
  • 🚪 Logout

docs.title: 2. Sidebar: Collection Tree
docs.text: The sidebar organizes all your API requests in a tree structure.

docs.title: Collections Tab
docs.text: View and manage your collections: - Collections: Top-level containers - Folders: Group related requests - Requests: Individual API endpoints

docs.title: History Tab
docs.text: Recent request executions with: - Timestamp - HTTP method and URL - Status code - Response time

docs.title: Search Tab
docs.text: Quickly find requests by: - Name - URL - Method - Tags

docs.title: Collection Tree Actions
docs.text:
Collection ActionsFolder ActionsRequest Actions

Right-click a collection to:

  • ✏️ Rename Collection
  • 📁 Add Folder
  • 📄 Add Request
  • ⚙️ Collection Settings
  • ▶️ Run Collection
  • 📤 Export Collection
  • ❌ Delete Collection

Right-click a folder to:

  • ✏️ Rename Folder
  • 📁 Add Subfolder
  • 📄 Add Request
  • ▶️ Run Folder
  • 🔼 Move Up/Down
  • ❌ Delete Folder

Right-click a request to:

  • ✏️ Rename Request
  • 📋 Duplicate Request
  • 🔼 Move Up/Down
  • 📁 Move to Folder
  • ❌ Delete Request

docs.title: Creating a Collection
docs.text:
1. Click + New button in sidebar
  2. Select New Collection
  3. Enter collection name
  4. (Optional) Add description
  5. Click Create

docs.title: 3. Request Builder
docs.text: The request builder is where you configure your API requests.

docs.title: URL Bar
docs.text:
Components: - HTTP Method Dropdown: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, etc. - URL Input: Enter endpoint URL (supports {{variables}}) - Send Button: Execute the request - Save Button: Save request changes

Example: 

GET  https://{{baseUrl}}/api/users/{{userId}}  [Send] [Save]

docs.title: Request Tabs
docs.text: The request builder has multiple tabs for different configuration aspects:

docs.title: Params Tab
docs.text:
Add query parameters that append to the URL:

Key Value Description page 1 Page number limit 10 Items per page sort name Sort field

Resulting URL: https://api.example.com/users?page=1&limit=10&sort=name

Variable Support

Use {{variableName}} in both keys and values to use environment variables.

docs.title: Headers Tab
docs.text:
Add HTTP headers to your request:

Key Value Description Content-Type application/json Request body format Authorization Bearer {{token}} Auth token X-API-Key {{apiKey}} API key

Common Headers: - Accept: Desired response format - User-Agent: Client identification - Cache-Control: Caching behavior - X-Custom-Header: Custom business logic

docs.title: Body Tab
docs.text:
Configure the request body (for POST, PUT, PATCH):

Body Types:

JSONForm DataRawBinary 
{
  "name": "{{userName}}",
  "email": "user@example.com",
  "role": "admin"
}
  • Syntax highlighting
  • Auto-formatting with Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + F
  • Variable interpolation

Key Value Type name John Doe Text avatar [Choose File] File age 30 Text 
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<user>
  <name>{{userName}}</name>
  <email>user@example.com</email>
</user>

Supports: XML, HTML, Plain Text, JavaScript

Upload a file directly as the request body

  • Click Select File
  • Choose file from disk
  • File sent as raw binary data

docs.title: Auth Tab
docs.text:
Configure authentication for the request:

Auth Types: - Bearer Token: JWT or API tokens - Basic Auth: Username and password - API Key: Custom key location and value - OAuth 2.0: OAuth flow configuration - AWS Signature V4: AWS service authentication - Digest Auth: Challenge-response authentication - No Auth: Inherit from collection or none

Learn more about authentication →

docs.title: Scripts Tab
docs.text:
Pre-Request Script:

JavaScript code executed before sending the request:

// Generate timestamp
pm.environment.set('timestamp', Date.now());

// Set dynamic token
const token = pm.environment.get('refreshToken');
pm.environment.set('authToken', token);

// Log for debugging
console.log('Request will be sent to:', pm.request.url);

Test Script:

JavaScript code executed after receiving the response:

// Validate status code
pm.test("Status is 200", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

// Validate response body
pm.test("Response has user data", function() {
    const data = pm.response.json();
    pm.expect(data).to.have.property('id');
    pm.expect(data.email).to.be.a('string');
});

// Extract data for next request
const userId = pm.response.json().id;
pm.environment.set('userId', userId);

View complete scripting API →

docs.title: 4. Response Viewer
docs.text: After sending a request, the response appears in the bottom section.

docs.title: Body Tab
docs.text:
View the response body in different formats:

View Options: - Pretty: Formatted JSON/XML with syntax highlighting - Raw: Unformatted text - Preview: Rendered HTML (for HTML responses)

Features: - 🔍 Search within response - 📋 Copy to clipboard - 💾 Save to file - 🔄 Auto-format

docs.title: Headers Tab
docs.text:
View all response headers:

Header Value content-type application/json; charset=utf-8 content-length 1234 cache-control no-cache x-ratelimit-remaining 99

docs.title: Cookies Tab
docs.text:
View cookies set by the server:

Name Value Domain Path Expires session_id abc123... .example.com / Session user_pref theme=dark .example.com / 7 days

docs.title: Test Results Tab
docs.text:
View test script execution results:

Passing TestsFailing Tests

✅ Status is 200 (15ms) ✅ Response has user data (3ms) ✅ Email format is valid (2ms)

3/3 tests passed

❌ Status is 200 (12ms) 

Expected: 200
Actual: 404

✅ Response has error message (2ms)

1/2 tests passed

docs.title: Console Tab
docs.text:
View console logs from scripts:

[Pre-Request] Request URL: https://api.example.com/users
[Pre-Request] Timestamp: 1704398400000
[Test] Response time: 245ms
[Test] User ID extracted: 12345

docs.title: 5. Status Bar
docs.text:
The status bar shows request execution details:

Left Side: - Status Code: 200 OK, 404 Not Found, etc. - Response Time: 245ms - Response Size: 1.2 KB

Right Side: - Request History: Jump to previous requests - Environment: Current active environment - Save Status: Auto-save indicator

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts
docs.text: Work faster with these shortcuts:

docs.title: Global Shortcuts
docs.text: Action Mac Windows/Linux Send Request Cmd + Enter Ctrl + Enter Save Request Cmd + S Ctrl + S New Request Cmd + N Ctrl + N Search Collections Cmd + P Ctrl + P Toggle Sidebar Cmd + B Ctrl + B

docs.title: Editor Shortcuts
docs.text:
Action Mac Windows/Linux Format JSON Cmd + Shift + F Ctrl + Shift + F Find in Editor Cmd + F Ctrl + F Replace in Editor Cmd + H Ctrl + H Toggle Comment Cmd + / Ctrl + /

View all keyboard shortcuts →

docs.title: Theme Options
docs.text:
• Light Mode: Default bright theme
  • Dark Mode: Eye-friendly dark theme
  • Auto: Follows system preference

To change theme: 1. Click user menu (top right) 2. Select Settings 3. Choose Appearance 4. Select theme

docs.title: Layout Options
docs.text:
• Sidebar Position: Left (default) or Right
  • Response Panel: Bottom (default) or Right
  • Font Size: Small, Medium, Large
  • Font Family: Monospace fonts for code

docs.text:
Now that you know your way around the interface:

docs.title: What is Simba?
docs.text: Simba is a modern, open-source API testing tool designed for developers and QA engineers. Built with React and Node.js, Simba provides a powerful yet intuitive interface for testing REST APIs, GraphQL endpoints, and WebSocket connections.

docs.title: 🎯 Purpose-Built for Modern APIs
docs.text:
Simba is designed from the ground up to handle modern API architectures:

  • REST API Testing - Full HTTP method support with advanced request/response handling
  • GraphQL Support - Native GraphQL with schema introspection and query validation
  • WebSocket Testing - Real-time connection testing and message handling
  • Scripting & Automation - JavaScript-based pre-request and test scripts

docs.title: Workspace-Based Organization
docs.text: Organize your API testing projects into isolated workspaces, each with its own collections, environments, and team members.

docs.title: Collection Management
docs.text: Group related requests into collections and folders, making it easy to organize and run batch tests.

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Manage multiple environments (Dev, Staging, Production) with variable substitution using {{variableName}} syntax.

docs.title: Pre-Request & Test Scripts
docs.text: Write JavaScript code to: - Set up authentication tokens - Generate dynamic data - Validate responses with assertions - Chain requests together

docs.title: Collection Runner
docs.text: Execute entire collections or folders with: - Sequential request execution - Data-driven testing from CSV/JSON files - Automated test reporting - Detailed execution logs

docs.title: Request History
docs.text: Track every request execution with full request/response details for debugging and auditing.

docs.title: Team Collaboration
docs.text: Share workspaces with team members, control access permissions, and import/export collections.

docs.title: Simba vs. Postman
docs.text: Feature Simba Postman Open Source ✅ Yes ❌ No Self-Hosted ✅ Yes ❌ Cloud only REST API ✅ Full support ✅ Full support GraphQL ✅ Native support ✅ Native support WebSocket ✅ Built-in ⚠️ Via extensions Scripting ✅ JavaScript (pm API) ✅ JavaScript (pm API) Collection Runner ✅ Yes ✅ Yes Data-Driven Testing ✅ CSV/JSON ✅ CSV/JSON Environments ✅ Unlimited ⚠️ Limited on free tier Team Workspaces ✅ Unlimited ⚠️ Limited on free tier Database 🔒 PostgreSQL + MongoDB ☁️ Cloud-based Pricing 💰 Free (self-hosted) 💰 Paid plans

docs.title: Developers
docs.text:
• Test APIs during development
  • Debug request/response issues
  • Automate API testing with scripts
  • Share collections with team members

docs.title: QA Engineers
docs.text:
• Validate API behavior across environments
  • Run regression test suites
  • Generate test reports for stakeholders
  • Perform data-driven testing

docs.title: DevOps Teams
docs.text:
• Integrate with CI/CD pipelines
  • Monitor API health in different environments
  • Maintain API testing infrastructure
  • Control sensitive data with self-hosting

docs.title: API Consumers
docs.text:
• Explore third-party APIs
  • Test authentication flows
  • Validate webhook endpoints
  • Document API usage patterns

docs.title: 1. Create a Workspace
docs.text: Workspaces are isolated containers for your API testing projects. Each workspace has its own collections, environments, and team members.

docs.title: 2. Organize Requests into Collections
docs.text: Group related API requests into collections and folders. Collections can contain: - Individual requests - Nested folders - Shared variables - Pre-request and test scripts

docs.title: 3. Configure Environments
docs.text: Define environment-specific variables (URLs, tokens, credentials) for Dev, Staging, and Production environments.

docs.title: 4. Build and Execute Requests
docs.text: Use the request builder to: - Set HTTP method and URL - Add query parameters and headers - Configure request body (JSON, form-data, raw) - Set up authentication - Write pre-request scripts - Define test assertions

docs.title: 5. Validate Responses
docs.text: View response details: - Status code and time - Response headers and cookies - Body (formatted JSON, XML, HTML) - Test results

docs.title: 6. Automate with Collection Runner
docs.text: Run entire collections with: - Sequential execution - Data-driven iterations - Test result aggregation - Export reports

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Simba uses a modern, dual-database architecture:

Frontend (React + TypeScript) - Vite-based build system - Zustand for state management - Monaco Editor for code editing - Tailwind CSS for styling

Backend (Node.js + Express + TypeScript) - RESTful API architecture - Prisma ORM for PostgreSQL (structured data) - Mongoose ODM for MongoDB (flexible documents) - JWT-based authentication - Request execution engine with script sandboxing

Databases - PostgreSQL - User accounts, workspaces, collections, request metadata - MongoDB - Large request/response bodies, headers, scripts

This hybrid approach optimizes for both relational data integrity and flexible document storage.

docs.title: Minimum Requirements
docs.text:
• OS: Windows 10+, macOS 10.14+, Linux (Ubuntu 18.04+)
  • RAM: 4GB
  • Disk Space: 1GB
  • Node.js: 18+
  • Databases: PostgreSQL 14+ and MongoDB 5+

docs.title: Recommended Requirements
docs.text:
• RAM: 8GB or more
  • Disk Space: 5GB
  • Node.js: 20 LTS
  • Databases: PostgreSQL 16 and MongoDB 7

docs.title: Self-Hosted Data
docs.text: All your API requests, responses, and sensitive data stay on your infrastructure. No cloud sync, no third-party access.

docs.title: Authentication Options
docs.text: Simba supports multiple authentication methods: - Bearer Token - Basic Auth - API Key - OAuth 2.0 - AWS Signature V4 - Digest Auth

docs.title: Script Sandboxing
docs.text: Pre-request and test scripts run in a sandboxed JavaScript environment with limited access to system resources.

docs.title: Database Security
docs.text:
• Passwords hashed with bcrypt
  • JWT tokens for session management
  • Environment variables for sensitive configuration
  • Database connection security with SSL/TLS support

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Getting Started: Step-by-step tutorials
  • Core Concepts: Understand workspaces, collections, and environments
  • Request Guides: Detailed guides for REST, GraphQL, and WebSocket
  • API Reference: Complete scripting API documentation

docs.title: Community & Support
docs.text:
• GitHub Issues: Report bugs and request features
  • Email Support: support@cdw.com
  • Contributing: See our contribution guidelines

docs.title: License
docs.text: Simba is open-source software licensed under the MIT License. You are free to use, modify, and distribute the software for commercial and non-commercial purposes.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get up and running with Simba in 5 minutes! This guide covers the essential steps to start testing APIs.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you begin, ensure:

  • ✅ Simba is installed and running (Installation Guide)
  • ✅ You can access Simba at http://localhost:5173
  • ✅ Backend server is running on http://localhost:5000

docs.title: Step 1: Create an Account
docs.text:
1. Open Simba in your browser: http://localhost:5173
  2. Click Sign Up
  3. Enter your details:
  4. Name: Your full name
  5. Email: Your email address
  6. Password: Strong password (8+ characters)
  7. Click Create Account
  8. You'll be automatically logged in

Quick Login

If you already have an account, click Login and enter your credentials.

docs.title: Step 2: Create Your First Workspace
docs.text:
Workspaces organize your API testing projects.

To create a workspace:

  1. You'll see the Create Workspace dialog on first login
  2. Enter workspace details:
  3. Name: e.g., "My API Project"
  4. Description: e.g., "Testing the Users API"
  5. Click Create Workspace

Your new workspace is now active and ready to use!

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Collection
docs.text:
Collections group related API requests together.

To create a collection:

  1. Click + New in the sidebar
  2. Select New Collection
  3. Enter collection details:
  4. Name: e.g., "Users API"
  5. Description: "User management endpoints"
  6. Click Create

Your collection appears in the sidebar!

docs.title: Step 4: Create Your First Request
docs.text:
To create a request:

  1. Right-click your collection in the sidebar
  2. Select Add Request
  3. Enter request details:
  4. Name: e.g., "Get All Users"
  5. Method: GET
  6. URL: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users
  7. Click Create

The request builder opens automatically.

docs.title: Step 5: Send the Request
docs.text:
To send your first API request:

  1. Verify the URL is correct: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users
  2. Method should be GET
  3. Click the Send button (or press Cmd/Ctrl + Enter)

That's it! You've sent your first API request.

docs.title: Step 6: View the Response
docs.text:
After sending, the response appears below:

Response Details: - Status: 200 OK ✅ - Time: Response time in milliseconds - Size: Response size in KB

Response Body: 

[
  {
    "id": 1,
    "name": "Leanne Graham",
    "username": "Bret",
    "email": "Sincere@april.biz",
    "address": {
      "street": "Kulas Light",
      "city": "Gwenborough"
    }
  }
  // ... more users
]

Response Tabs: - Body: View the response data (JSON, XML, HTML) - Headers: See response headers - Cookies: View set cookies - Test Results: See test outcomes (if you have tests)

docs.title: Step 7: Add a Test Script
docs.text:
Let's add a simple test to validate the response.

To add a test:

  1. Click the Scripts tab in the request builder
  2. Select Test Script (post-response)
  3. Add this code:
// Test 1: Verify status code
pm.test("Status code is 200", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

// Test 2: Verify response is an array
pm.test("Response is an array", function() {
    const users = pm.response.json();
    pm.expect(users).to.be.an('array');
});

// Test 3: Verify array has users
pm.test("Response has users", function() {
    const users = pm.response.json();
    pm.expect(users.length).to.be.above(0);
});

// Test 4: User has required fields
pm.test("User has required fields", function() {
    const user = pm.response.json()[0];
    pm.expect(user).to.have.property('id');
    pm.expect(user).to.have.property('name');
    pm.expect(user).to.have.property('email');
});
  1. Click Send again to run the tests

Result: All 4 tests pass! ✅

docs.title: Step 8: Create a POST Request
docs.text:
Let's create a more complex request that sends data.

To create a POST request:

  1. Right-click your collection
  2. Select Add Request
  3. Enter details:
  4. Name: "Create User"
  5. Method: POST
  6. URL: https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users
  7. Click Create

docs.title: Configure the POST Request
docs.text:
1. Add Headers: - Go to Headers tab - Add header: - Key: Content-Type - Value: application/json

2. Add Request Body: - Go to Body tab - Select JSON format - Add this JSON:

{
  "name": "John Doe",
  "username": "johndoe",
  "email": "john.doe@example.com",
  "phone": "1-555-123-4567",
  "website": "johndoe.com"
}

3. Add Test: - Go to Scripts tab - Add this test:

pm.test("User created successfully", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.status(201);
});

pm.test("Response has ID", function() {
    const data = pm.response.json();
    pm.expect(data).to.have.property('id');
});

4. Send the Request: - Click Send

Response: 

{
  "id": 11,
  "name": "John Doe",
  "username": "johndoe",
  "email": "john.doe@example.com",
  "phone": "1-555-123-4567",
  "website": "johndoe.com"
}

✅ Status: 201 Created ✅ Tests: 2/2 passed

docs.title: Step 9: Use Environment Variables
docs.text: Environment variables help manage different environments (Dev, Staging, Production).

docs.title: Create an Environment
docs.text:
To create an environment:

  1. Click the Environment dropdown (top center)
  2. Select + Create Environment
  3. Enter environment name: "Development"
  4. Add variables:
Variable Value baseUrl https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com apiKey your-dev-api-key
  1. Click Save
  2. Set as Active Environment

docs.title: Use Variables in Requests
docs.text:
Update your request URL:

Before: 

https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users

After: 

{{baseUrl}}/users

Benefits: - ✅ Switch environments easily - ✅ Share collections without hardcoded URLs - ✅ Manage sensitive data (API keys)

docs.title: Step 10: View Request History
docs.text:
Every request you send is saved to history.

To view history:

  1. Click History tab in sidebar
  2. See all previous requests with:
  3. Timestamp
  4. Method and URL
  5. Status code
  6. Response time
  7. Click any entry to re-execute it

History is useful for: - 🔄 Re-running previous requests - 🐛 Debugging API issues - 📊 Comparing responses over time - 📝 Auditing API usage

docs.text:

  • :material-school:{ .lg .middle } Complete Tutorials

    Follow step-by-step tutorials for common use cases

    :octicons-arrow-right-24: View Tutorials

docs.title: Can't Access Simba
docs.text:
Problem: Browser shows "Cannot connect" error

Solutions: 1. Verify servers are running: 

# Backend should show: Server listening on port 5000
# Frontend should show: Local: http://localhost:5173
2. Check firewall settings 3. Try a different browser

docs.title: Request Fails with CORS Error
docs.text:
Problem: Console shows CORS error

Solutions: 1. Verify backend FRONTEND_URL in .env matches your frontend URL 2. Check API server allows your origin 3. Use backend proxy for third-party APIs

docs.title: Tests Not Running
docs.text:
Problem: Test results tab shows "No tests run"

Solutions: 1. Verify test script uses pm.test() function 2. Check for syntax errors in script 3. Look at Console tab for error messages

docs.title: Tips for Success
docs.text:
Save Frequently

Click Save (Cmd/Ctrl + S) after making changes to requests.

Use Collections

Group related requests into collections and folders for better organization.

Name Requests Descriptively

Use clear names like "Get User by ID" instead of "Request 1".

Add Descriptions

Document what each request does in the description field.

Leverage Variables

Use {{variables}} for anything that changes between environments.

Write Tests Early

Add test scripts as you build requests to catch issues immediately.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
Need more help?

  • 📖 Full Documentation - Complete guides and references
  • 🐛 GitHub Issues - Report bugs
  • 📧 Email Support - support@cdw.com
  • ❓ FAQ - Common questions

Happy API testing! 🚀

docs.title: Scripting API Reference
docs.text: Complete reference for writing pre-request and test scripts in Simba.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Simba supports JavaScript scripting for: - Pre-request scripts: Run before sending the request - Test scripts: Run after receiving the response

Scripts use the pm.* API, compatible with Postman scripts.

docs.title: pm.request
docs.text:
Access the current request.

// Get request URL
const url = pm.request.url.toString();

// Get request method
const method = pm.request.method;

// Get headers
const headers = pm.request.headers;

docs.title: pm.response
docs.text:
Access the response (only in test scripts).

// Get status code
const status = pm.response.code;

// Get response time (ms)
const time = pm.response.responseTime;

// Get response body as JSON
const jsonData = pm.response.json();

// Get response body as text
const text = pm.response.text();

// Get response headers
const headers = pm.response.headers;

docs.title: pm.environment
docs.text:
Work with environment variables.

// Get environment variable
const apiKey = pm.environment.get(\"apiKey\");

// Set environment variable
pm.environment.set(\"token\", \"abc123\");

// Check if variable exists
if (pm.environment.has(\"userId\")) {
    // Do something
}

// Remove variable
pm.environment.unset(\"tempVar\");

docs.title: pm.variables
docs.text:
Access all variables (environment, collection, global).

// Get variable (searches all scopes)
const value = pm.variables.get(\"variableName\");

// Set variable
pm.variables.set(\"tempValue\", \"test\");

docs.title: pm.globals
docs.text:
Work with global variables.

// Get global variable
const baseUrl = pm.globals.get(\"baseUrl\");

// Set global variable
pm.globals.set(\"timestamp\", Date.now());

// Remove global variable
pm.globals.unset(\"oldVar\");

docs.title: pm.collectionVariables
docs.text:
Work with collection-level variables.

// Get collection variable
const apiVersion = pm.collectionVariables.get(\"version\");

// Set collection variable
pm.collectionVariables.set(\"lastRun\", new Date().toISOString());

docs.title: pm.test()
docs.text:
Define a test.

pm.test(\"Status code is 200\", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

docs.title: pm.expect()
docs.text:
Chai assertion library.

// Basic assertions
pm.expect(1 + 1).to.equal(2);
pm.expect(\"hello\").to.be.a(\"string\");
pm.expect([1, 2, 3]).to.include(2);

// Response assertions
const jsonData = pm.response.json();
pm.expect(jsonData.name).to.exist;
pm.expect(jsonData.age).to.be.above(18);
pm.expect(jsonData.email).to.match(/^.+@.+\\..+$/);

docs.title: Common Assertions
docs.text:
// Status code
pm.response.to.have.status(200);
pm.response.to.have.status(\"OK\");

// Headers
pm.response.to.have.header(\"Content-Type\");
pm.response.to.have.header(\"Content-Type\", \"application/json\");

// Response body
pm.response.to.be.json;
pm.response.to.be.ok;  // Status 200-299

// Response time
pm.expect(pm.response.responseTime).to.be.below(500);

docs.title: Set Dynamic Timestamp
docs.text:
// Add current timestamp to environment
const timestamp = Date.now();
pm.environment.set(\"timestamp\", timestamp);

docs.title: Generate Random Data
docs.text:
// Generate random user ID
const randomId = Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000);
pm.environment.set(\"userId\", randomId);

// Generate UUID
const uuid = 'xxxxxxxx-xxxx-4xxx-yxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx'.replace(/[xy]/g, function(c) {
    const r = Math.random() * 16 | 0;
    const v = c === 'x' ? r : (r & 0x3 | 0x8);
    return v.toString(16);
});
pm.environment.set(\"requestId\", uuid);

docs.title: Calculate Authentication Hash
docs.text:
// Generate HMAC signature
const apiKey = pm.environment.get(\"apiKey\");
const secret = pm.environment.get(\"apiSecret\");
const timestamp = Date.now();

// Simple hash (in production, use proper HMAC)
const signature = btoa(`${apiKey}:${timestamp}:${secret}`);
pm.environment.set(\"signature\", signature);

docs.title: Basic Response Validation
docs.text:
// Verify status code
pm.test(\"Status code is 200\", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.status(200);
});

// Verify response time
pm.test(\"Response time < 500ms\", function() {
    pm.expect(pm.response.responseTime).to.be.below(500);
});

// Verify content type
pm.test(\"Content-Type is JSON\", function() {
    pm.response.to.have.header(\"Content-Type\", /application\\/json/);
});

docs.title: JSON Response Validation
docs.text:
pm.test(\"Response has required fields\", function() {
    const jsonData = pm.response.json();

    pm.expect(jsonData).to.have.property(\"id\");
    pm.expect(jsonData).to.have.property(\"name\");
    pm.expect(jsonData).to.have.property(\"email\");

    pm.expect(jsonData.id).to.be.a(\"number\");
    pm.expect(jsonData.name).to.be.a(\"string\");
    pm.expect(jsonData.email).to.match(/^.+@.+\\..+$/);
});

docs.title: Array Response Validation
docs.text:
pm.test(\"Response is an array with items\", function() {
    const jsonData = pm.response.json();

    pm.expect(jsonData).to.be.an(\"array\");
    pm.expect(jsonData).to.have.length.above(0);

    // Check first item structure
    const firstItem = jsonData[0];
    pm.expect(firstItem).to.have.property(\"id\");
    pm.expect(firstItem).to.have.property(\"title\");
});

docs.title: Save Response Data for Next Request
docs.text:
pm.test(\"Extract and save token\", function() {
    const jsonData = pm.response.json();

    // Save token for use in subsequent requests
    pm.environment.set(\"authToken\", jsonData.token);

    // Save user ID
    pm.environment.set(\"currentUserId\", jsonData.user.id);
});

docs.title: Schema Validation
docs.text:
pm.test(\"Response matches schema\", function() {
    const jsonData = pm.response.json();

    const schema = {
        type: \"object\",
        required: [\"id\", \"name\", \"email\"],
        properties: {
            id: { type: \"number\" },
            name: { type: \"string\", minLength: 1 },
            email: { type: \"string\", format: \"email\" },
            age: { type: \"number\", minimum: 0 }
        }
    };

    pm.expect(tv4.validate(jsonData, schema)).to.be.true;
});

docs.title: Conditional Logic
docs.text:
// Different assertions based on environment
const env = pm.environment.get(\"environment\");

if (env === \"production\") {
    pm.test(\"Production - Response must be fast\", function() {
        pm.expect(pm.response.responseTime).to.be.below(200);
    });
} else {
    pm.test(\"Dev - Response time OK\", function() {
        pm.expect(pm.response.responseTime).to.be.below(1000);
    });
}

docs.title: Retry Logic (Pre-request)
docs.text:
// Increment retry counter
let retryCount = pm.environment.get(\"retryCount\") || 0;
pm.environment.set(\"retryCount\", retryCount + 1);

// Check max retries
if (retryCount > 3) {
    console.error(\"Max retries exceeded\");
    pm.environment.unset(\"retryCount\");
}

docs.title: Dynamic URL Construction
docs.text:
// Build URL from parts
const baseUrl = pm.environment.get(\"baseUrl\");
const version = pm.collectionVariables.get(\"apiVersion\");
const endpoint = \"users\";

const fullUrl = `${baseUrl}/api/${version}/${endpoint}`;
pm.environment.set(\"requestUrl\", fullUrl);

docs.title: Console Output
docs.text:
// Debug output
console.log(\"Current environment:\", pm.environment.name);
console.log(\"Response data:\", pm.response.json());

// Variable inspection
console.log(\"All variables:\", pm.variables.toObject());

View console logs in the Response → Console tab.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
✅ Keep scripts simple - Complex logic should be in your application ✅ Use descriptive test names - Makes debugging easier ✅ Clean up temp variables - Use pm.environment.unset() after use ✅ Log important data - Use console.log() for debugging ✅ Handle errors - Use try/catch for robust scripts 

try {
    const jsonData = pm.response.json();
    pm.environment.set(\"userId\", jsonData.id);
} catch (error) {
    console.error(\"Failed to parse response:\", error);
}

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Pre-request Scripts Guide
  • Test Scripts Guide

docs.title: GraphQL Mutations
docs.text: Learn how to create, update, and delete data with GraphQL mutations.

docs.title: What are GraphQL Mutations?
docs.text: Mutations are write operations in GraphQL (similar to POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE in REST). They allow you to: - Create new data - Update existing data - Delete data - Trigger server-side actions

docs.title: Mutation vs Query
docs.text: Aspect Query Mutation Purpose Read data Write data Side effects None Modifies server state Execution Parallel (can be) Sequential (guaranteed) Convention query { ... } mutation { ... } REST equivalent GET POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE

docs.title: Simple Mutation
docs.text:
mutation {
  createUser(name: "John Doe", email: "john@example.com") {
    id
    name
    email
    createdAt
  }
}

Response: 

{
  "data": {
    "createUser": {
      "id": "123",
      "name": "John Doe",
      "email": "john@example.com",
      "createdAt": "2024-04-16T10:00:00Z"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Named Mutation
docs.text:
Always name your mutations:

mutation CreateNewUser {
  createUser(name: "John Doe", email: "john@example.com") {
    id
    name
  }
}

docs.title: Mutation with Variables (Recommended)
docs.text:
mutation CreateUser($name: String!, $email: String!) {
  createUser(name: $name, email: $email) {
    id
    name
    email
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "name": "John Doe",
  "email": "john@example.com"
}

docs.title: Create (POST equivalent)
docs.text:
mutation CreatePost($input: CreatePostInput!) {
  createPost(input: $input) {
    id
    title
    content
    author {
      name
    }
    createdAt
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "input": {
    "title": "Getting Started with GraphQL",
    "content": "GraphQL is a query language...",
    "authorId": "123",
    "tags": ["graphql", "tutorial"]
  }
}

docs.title: Update (PUT/PATCH equivalent)
docs.text:
mutation UpdateUser($id: ID!, $input: UpdateUserInput!) {
  updateUser(id: $id, input: $input) {
    id
    name
    email
    updatedAt
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "id": "123",
  "input": {
    "name": "Jane Doe",
    "bio": "GraphQL enthusiast"
  }
}

docs.title: Delete (DELETE equivalent)
docs.text:
mutation DeletePost($id: ID!) {
  deletePost(id: $id) {
    success
    message
    deletedId
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "id": "456"
}

docs.title: Upsert (Create or Update)
docs.text:
mutation UpsertUser($email: String!, $input: UserInput!) {
  upsertUser(email: $email, input: $input) {
    id
    name
    email
    isNew
  }
}

docs.title: Simple Inputs
docs.text:
mutation CreateTag($name: String!, $color: String!) {
  createTag(name: $name, color: $color) {
    id
    name
    color
  }
}

docs.title: Complex Input Objects
docs.text:
# Schema defines input type
input CreateUserInput {
  name: String!
  email: String!
  profile: ProfileInput
  preferences: PreferencesInput
}

input ProfileInput {
  bio: String
  website: String
  location: String
}

input PreferencesInput {
  theme: String
  language: String
  notifications: Boolean
}

Mutation: 

mutation CreateUser($input: CreateUserInput!) {
  createUser(input: $input) {
    id
    name
    profile {
      bio
      website
    }
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "input": {
    "name": "John Doe",
    "email": "john@example.com",
    "profile": {
      "bio": "Software developer",
      "website": "https://johndoe.com"
    },
    "preferences": {
      "theme": "dark",
      "notifications": true
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Lists in Inputs
docs.text:
mutation CreatePost($input: CreatePostInput!) {
  createPost(input: $input) {
    id
    title
    tags {
      name
    }
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "input": {
    "title": "My Post",
    "tags": ["graphql", "api", "tutorial"],
    "collaboratorIds": ["user1", "user2"]
  }
}

docs.title: Multiple Mutations
docs.text:
You can execute multiple mutations in one request. They run sequentially (not parallel):

mutation CreateUserAndPost {
  # First: Create user
  user: createUser(name: "John", email: "john@example.com") {
    id
    name
  }

  # Then: Create post (can't use user.id in same request)
  post: createPost(title: "My First Post", authorId: "123") {
    id
    title
  }
}

Response: 

{
  "data": {
    "user": {
      "id": "123",
      "name": "John"
    },
    "post": {
      "id": "456",
      "title": "My First Post"
    }
  }
}

Important: You can't use the result of the first mutation as input to the second in the same request. You need two separate requests.

docs.title: GitHub API: Create Repository
docs.text:
mutation CreateRepo($input: CreateRepositoryInput!) {
  createRepository(input: $input) {
    repository {
      id
      name
      url
      description
      visibility
      owner {
        login
      }
    }
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "input": {
    "name": "my-new-repo",
    "description": "A test repository",
    "visibility": "PUBLIC",
    "hasIssuesEnabled": true,
    "hasWikiEnabled": false
  }
}

Test: 

pm.test("Repository created", () => {
  const data = pm.response.json().data;
  pm.expect(data.createRepository).to.exist;
  pm.expect(data.createRepository.repository).to.exist;
});

pm.test("Repository has correct name", () => {
  const repo = pm.response.json().data.createRepository.repository;
  pm.expect(repo.name).to.equal("my-new-repo");
});

pm.test("No GraphQL errors", () => {
  const json = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(json.errors).to.be.undefined;
});

// Save repo ID for cleanup
const repoId = pm.response.json().data.createRepository.repository.id;
pm.environment.set("createdRepoId", repoId);

docs.title: GitHub API: Add Star
docs.text:
mutation AddStar($input: AddStarInput!) {
  addStar(input: $input) {
    starrable {
      ... on Repository {
        name
        stargazerCount
      }
    }
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "input": {
    "starrableId": "MDEwOlJlcG9zaXRvcnkxMDI3MDI1MA=="
  }
}

docs.title: GitHub API: Create Issue
docs.text:
mutation CreateIssue($input: CreateIssueInput!) {
  createIssue(input: $input) {
    issue {
      id
      number
      title
      url
      state
      author {
        login
      }
    }
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "input": {
    "repositoryId": "R_kgDOBP7V8g",
    "title": "Bug: Login not working",
    "body": "When I try to login, I get a 500 error.",
    "labelIds": ["LA_kwDOBP7V8s8AAAABP3YTdA"]
  }
}

Test: 

pm.test("Issue created", () => {
  const issue = pm.response.json().data.createIssue.issue;
  pm.expect(issue).to.exist;
  pm.expect(issue.number).to.be.a('number');
  pm.expect(issue.state).to.equal('OPEN');
});

// Save issue number for follow-up
const issueNumber = pm.response.json().data.createIssue.issue.number;
pm.environment.set("lastIssueNumber", issueNumber);

docs.title: Validation Errors
docs.text:
mutation CreateUser($input: CreateUserInput!) {
  createUser(input: $input) {
    id
    name
  }
}

Variables (invalid email): 

{
  "input": {
    "name": "John",
    "email": "not-an-email"
  }
}

Response: 

{
  "errors": [
    {
      "message": "Validation error",
      "extensions": {
        "code": "BAD_USER_INPUT",
        "field": "email",
        "validationError": "Invalid email format"
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Business Logic Errors
docs.text:
mutation DeleteUser($id: ID!) {
  deleteUser(id: $id) {
    success
    message
  }
}

Response (user has posts): 

{
  "data": {
    "deleteUser": null
  },
  "errors": [
    {
      "message": "Cannot delete user with existing posts",
      "extensions": {
        "code": "BUSINESS_LOGIC_ERROR",
        "userId": "123",
        "postCount": 5
      }
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Testing Error Handling
docs.text:
pm.test("Handle validation errors", () => {
  const json = pm.response.json();

  if (json.errors) {
    const error = json.errors[0];

    // Check error structure
    pm.expect(error).to.have.property('message');
    pm.expect(error.extensions).to.exist;

    // Check error code
    pm.expect(error.extensions.code).to.be.oneOf([
      'BAD_USER_INPUT',
      'BUSINESS_LOGIC_ERROR',
      'UNAUTHENTICATED',
      'FORBIDDEN'
    ]);

    // Log for debugging
    console.log("Error:", error.message);
    console.log("Code:", error.extensions.code);
  }
});

docs.title: Complete Test Suite
docs.text:
// Test successful mutation
pm.test("Mutation successful", () => {
  pm.response.to.have.status(200);

  const json = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(json.errors).to.be.undefined;
  pm.expect(json.data.createUser).to.exist;
});

// Test returned data
pm.test("Created user has correct fields", () => {
  const user = pm.response.json().data.createUser;

  pm.expect(user).to.have.property('id');
  pm.expect(user.id).to.be.a('string').and.not.empty;

  pm.expect(user).to.have.property('name');
  pm.expect(user.name).to.equal(pm.variables.get('userName'));

  pm.expect(user).to.have.property('createdAt');
});

// Test data types
pm.test("Fields have correct types", () => {
  const user = pm.response.json().data.createUser;

  pm.expect(user.id).to.be.a('string');
  pm.expect(user.name).to.be.a('string');
  pm.expect(user.email).to.be.a('string');
  pm.expect(user.isActive).to.be.a('boolean');
});

// Test timestamp
pm.test("CreatedAt is recent", () => {
  const user = pm.response.json().data.createUser;
  const createdAt = new Date(user.createdAt);
  const now = new Date();
  const diff = now - createdAt;

  // Created within last 5 seconds
  pm.expect(diff).to.be.below(5000);
});

// Test business logic
pm.test("New user is active by default", () => {
  const user = pm.response.json().data.createUser;
  pm.expect(user.isActive).to.be.true;
});

// Save for cleanup or next request
const userId = pm.response.json().data.createUser.id;
pm.environment.set("lastCreatedUserId", userId);
pm.collectionVariables.set("testUserId", userId);

docs.title: Test Update Mutation
docs.text:
pm.test("Update successful", () => {
  const json = pm.response.json();
  pm.expect(json.errors).to.be.undefined;
  pm.expect(json.data.updateUser).to.exist;
});

pm.test("Only specified fields updated", () => {
  const user = pm.response.json().data.updateUser;

  // These should change
  pm.expect(user.name).to.equal("Updated Name");

  // These should not change
  pm.expect(user.id).to.equal(pm.variables.get('userId'));
  pm.expect(user.email).to.equal(pm.variables.get('userEmail'));
});

pm.test("UpdatedAt timestamp changed", () => {
  const user = pm.response.json().data.updateUser;
  const updatedAt = new Date(user.updatedAt);
  const createdAt = new Date(user.createdAt);

  pm.expect(updatedAt).to.be.above(createdAt);
});

docs.title: Test Delete Mutation
docs.text:
pm.test("Delete successful", () => {
  const result = pm.response.json().data.deleteUser;
  pm.expect(result.success).to.be.true;
  pm.expect(result.message).to.exist;
});

pm.test("Returns deleted ID", () => {
  const result = pm.response.json().data.deleteUser;
  pm.expect(result.deletedId).to.equal(pm.variables.get('userId'));
});

// Verify deletion in next request
pm.test("Cleanup environment", () => {
  pm.environment.unset("userId");
});

docs.title: Environment Variables in Mutations
docs.text:
mutation CreatePost($input: CreatePostInput!) {
  createPost(input: $input) {
    id
    title
    author {
      name
    }
  }
}

Variables (using Simba {{variables}}): 

{
  "input": {
    "title": "{{postTitle}}",
    "content": "{{postContent}}",
    "authorId": "{{currentUserId}}"
  }
}

docs.title: Pre-request Script to Generate Data
docs.text:
// Generate random data
const randomEmail = `user${Date.now()}@example.com`;
const randomName = `User ${Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000)}`;

// Set as variables
pm.variables.set('randomEmail', randomEmail);
pm.variables.set('randomName', randomName);

// Or set GraphQL variables directly
const variables = {
  input: {
    name: randomName,
    email: randomEmail,
    password: 'test123'
  }
};

pm.variables.set('graphqlVariables', JSON.stringify(variables));

Query using generated data: 

mutation CreateUser($input: CreateUserInput!) {
  createUser(input: $input) {
    id
    name
    email
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "input": {
    "name": "{{randomName}}",
    "email": "{{randomEmail}}",
    "password": "test123"
  }
}

docs.title: Optimistic Response
docs.text:
In client code (not Simba), you can optimistically update UI:

mutation LikePost($postId: ID!) {
  likePost(postId: $postId) {
    id
    likes
    isLikedByViewer
  }
}

docs.title: Batch Mutations
docs.text:
Some APIs support batch operations:

mutation CreateMultiplePosts($inputs: [CreatePostInput!]!) {
  createPosts(inputs: $inputs) {
    posts {
      id
      title
    }
    errors {
      index
      message
    }
  }
}

Variables: 

{
  "inputs": [
    { "title": "Post 1", "content": "..." },
    { "title": "Post 2", "content": "..." },
    { "title": "Post 3", "content": "..." }
  ]
}

docs.title: Conditional Mutations
docs.text:
Use directives for conditional execution:

mutation UpdateUser($userId: ID!, $input: UserInput!, $notify: Boolean!) {
  updateUser(id: $userId, input: $input) {
    id
    name

    # Only fetch notifications if needed
    notifications @include(if: $notify) {
      unreadCount
    }
  }
}

docs.title: 1. Always Use Variables
docs.text:
❌ Bad - Hard-coded values: 

mutation {
  createUser(name: "John", email: "john@example.com") {
    id
  }
}

✅ Good - Use variables: 

mutation CreateUser($name: String!, $email: String!) {
  createUser(name: $name, email: $email) {
    id
  }
}

docs.title: 2. Request Useful Data Back
docs.text:
❌ Bad - Only request success boolean: 

mutation CreatePost($input: CreatePostInput!) {
  createPost(input: $input) {
    success
  }
}

✅ Good - Request created object: 

mutation CreatePost($input: CreatePostInput!) {
  createPost(input: $input) {
    id
    title
    slug
    createdAt
    author {
      id
      name
    }
  }
}

docs.title: 3. Use Input Types
docs.text:
❌ Bad - Many separate arguments: 

mutation CreateUser(
  $name: String!
  $email: String!
  $bio: String
  $website: String
  $location: String
) {
  createUser(name: $name, email: $email, bio: $bio, ...) {
    id
  }
}

✅ Good - Single input object: 

mutation CreateUser($input: CreateUserInput!) {
  createUser(input: $input) {
    id
  }
}

docs.title: 4. Handle Errors Gracefully
docs.text:
const json = pm.response.json();

if (json.errors) {
  json.errors.forEach(error => {
    console.error(`Error: ${error.message}`);

    if (error.extensions) {
      console.error(`Code: ${error.extensions.code}`);
      console.error(`Details:`, error.extensions);
    }
  });

  pm.expect(json.errors).to.be.empty;
}

docs.title: 5. Clean Up Test Data
docs.text:
// After creating test data
const userId = pm.response.json().data.createUser.id;

// Add to cleanup list
const cleanup = pm.collectionVariables.get("cleanup") || [];
cleanup.push({ type: "user", id: userId });
pm.collectionVariables.set("cleanup", cleanup);

// In teardown request:
const cleanup = pm.collectionVariables.get("cleanup") || [];
cleanup.forEach(item => {
  // Delete each item
});

docs.title: Issue: Input Validation Failed
docs.text:
Error: 

{
  "errors": [{
    "message": "Variable \"$input\" got invalid value ...",
    "extensions": {
      "code": "BAD_USER_INPUT"
    }
  }]
}

Solutions: - Check input types match schema - Verify required fields are provided - Ensure type conversions (string vs number)

docs.title: Issue: Unauthorized
docs.text:
Error: 

{
  "errors": [{
    "message": "Not authorized",
    "extensions": {
      "code": "FORBIDDEN"
    }
  }]
}

Solutions: - Add authentication header - Check token is valid - Verify permissions

docs.title: Issue: Unique Constraint Violation
docs.text:
Error: 

{
  "errors": [{
    "message": "Email already exists",
    "extensions": {
      "code": "CONSTRAINT_VIOLATION",
      "field": "email"
    }
  }]
}

Solutions: - Use unique values (timestamp, UUID) - Check before creating - Use upsert instead of create

docs.title: Issue: Mutation Returned Null
docs.text:
Response: 

{
  "data": {
    "updateUser": null
  },
  "errors": [{
    "message": "User not found"
  }]
}

Solutions: - Verify ID exists - Check permissions - Resource might be deleted

docs.title: Create → Read → Update → Delete
docs.text:
1. Create User: 

mutation CreateUser {
  createUser(input: {
    name: "Test User"
    email: "test@example.com"
  }) {
    id
    name
  }
}

2. Read User: 

query GetUser($id: ID!) {
  user(id: $id) {
    id
    name
    email
    createdAt
  }
}

3. Update User: 

mutation UpdateUser($id: ID!, $input: UpdateUserInput!) {
  updateUser(id: $id, input: $input) {
    id
    name
    updatedAt
  }
}

4. Delete User: 

mutation DeleteUser($id: ID!) {
  deleteUser(id: $id) {
    success
    deletedId
  }
}

docs.title: Relationship Management
docs.text:
1. Create parent: 

mutation CreateUser {
  createUser(input: { name: "John" }) {
    id
  }
}

2. Create child: 

mutation CreatePost($authorId: ID!) {
  createPost(input: {
    title: "My Post"
    authorId: $authorId
  }) {
    id
    title
    author {
      name
    }
  }
}

3. Link existing: 

mutation AddPostTag($postId: ID!, $tagId: ID!) {
  addPostTag(postId: $postId, tagId: $tagId) {
    post {
      id
      tags {
        name
      }
    }
  }
}

docs.text:
• Variables - Master GraphQL variables
  • Schema Explorer - Discover mutation types
  • Queries - Read data after mutations
  • Tutorial - Complete workflow

docs.title: Related Topics
docs.text:
• GraphQL Overview
  • Test Scripts
  • Pre-request Scripts
